topics.title: Cardiac pacemakers: different modes of operation, external and implantable pacemakers, pacemaker standard codes. Need for cardiac pacemaker. Defibrillator: AC and DC defibrillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT741 - THERAPEUTIC EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cardiac pacemakers: different modes of operation, external and implantable pacemakers, pacemaker standard codes. Need for cardiac pacemaker. Defibrillator: AC and DC defibrillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Cardiac Pacemaker?

**Cardiac Pacemaker:** A battery-operated, electronic medical device designed to deliver precisely timed, low-energy electrical impulses (typically **0.5 mA to 10 mA** at **2.5 V to 5 V**, with a pulse width of **0.1 ms to 2 ms**) to the cardiac muscle (myocardium) in order to initiate and regulate the heart's rhythm when the body's natural intrinsic conduction system fails or becomes unreliable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Form):** A pacemaker is an implantable or external pulse generator that electrically stimulates the myocardium to maintain an adequate heart rate in patients with bradyarrhythmias, conduction blocks, or post-cardiac-surgery complications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mechanical Metronome for the Heart"

Imagine a drummer in a marching band who is supposed to maintain a steady beat for 100 musicians. Suddenly, the drummer becomes drowsy and starts losing the beat — sometimes too slow, sometimes irregular. The conductor's solution? Hand the drummer a **mechanical metronome** (an external device) that produces a click-click-click sound, forcing the drummer to synchronize with it.

In this analogy:
- **Drowsy drummer** = Sino-Atrial (SA) Node (the heart's natural pacemaker) malfunctioning
- **Musicians** = Atrial and Ventricular chambers of the heart
- **Metronome** = Artificial electronic pacemaker
- **Audible click** = Electrical pacing pulse (typically **2 V to 5 V**)
- **Synchronization** = Capturing the myocardium and triggering contraction

The pacemaker does NOT replace the heart's natural electrical activity entirely — it simply **assists, overrides, or backstops** it when the intrinsic rhythm drops below a safe threshold (commonly < **60 bpm** in resting adults).

## 1.3 Need for a Cardiac Pacemaker

The human heart has its own intrinsic conduction system: **SA Node → AV Node → Bundle of His → Bundle Branches → Purkinje Fibers**. When any link in this chain fails, life-threatening arrhythmias can occur. The clinical conditions warranting a pacemaker are:

| S.No. | Clinical Condition | Description | Typical Pacing Mode |
|-------|--------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Sick Sinus Syndrome (SSS)** | SA node fires too slowly or pauses | AAI, DDD |
| 2 | **Complete Heart Block (CHB)** | AV node fully blocked; atria & ventricles beat independently | DDD, VVI |
| 3 | **Symptomatic Bradycardia** | Resting HR < 40 bpm with syncope | VVI, DDD |
| 4 | **Atrial Fibrillation with Slow Ventricular Response** | AF + slow HR | VVI |
| 5 | **Post-MI Conduction Defects** | Heart attack damaging conduction pathways | DDD, VVI |
| 6 | **Cardiac Resynchronization Therapy (CRT)** | Bundle branch blocks causing dyssynchrony | BiV pacing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Perspective:** From a biomedical engineering standpoint, the pacemaker is the single most successful implantable electronic device in medical history, with over **1 million** implantations performed worldwide annually (data from peer-reviewed cardiology literature, knowledge cutoff 2026).

## 1.4 What is a Defibrillator?

**Defibrillator:** A life-saving therapeutic device that delivers a brief, high-energy electrical shock (typically **200 J to 360 J** for adults, **2 J to 4 J/kg** for pediatric patients) to the myocardium in order to **depolarize a critical mass of cardiac muscle simultaneously**, thereby terminating lethal arrhythmias such as Ventricular Fibrillation (VF) or pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia (VT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Distinction — Pacemaker vs. Defibrillator:**
> - **Pacemaker** → Low energy (~**25 μJ** to **50 μJ** per pulse), used to *initiate* missing beats (Bradycardia).
> - **Defibrillator** → High energy (~**200 J to 360 J**), used to *terminate* chaotic electrical activity (Tachyarrhythmias/VF).
> - Energy difference: A defibrillator delivers **~10 million times** more energy per shock than a pacemaker pulse.

## 1.5 Defibrillator Conceptual Analogy — "The Software Reboot"

Think of your computer when it freezes and the screen goes into chaos. No matter which key you press, nothing responds correctly. What do you do? You press the **hardware reset button** — a sudden, full power cut and restart that forces all software processes to terminate simultaneously, allowing the system to boot fresh.

Similarly, during Ventricular Fibrillation, the cardiac muscle cells fire in a chaotic, uncoordinated manner (like a frozen computer). The defibrillator delivers a **massive, synchronized electrical reset** to all cardiac cells at once, allowing the SA node (the natural "operating system") to reclaim control and resume normal rhythm.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Modes of Pacemaker Operation

Modern pacemakers operate in several distinct modes, each tailored to specific clinical needs. The modes are classified based on **which chambers are paced**, **which chambers are sensed**, and **how the device responds to sensed intrinsic activity**.

### 2.1.1 AOO / VOO / DOO — Asynchronous (Fixed-Rate) Mode

- **Paces:** A (atrium), V (ventricle), or D (both)
- **Senses:** **Nothing** (no sensing function)
- **Response:** **None** — fires at a programmed rate regardless of intrinsic activity
- **Use:** Emergency pacing, during surgery (electrocautery interference), magnet application
- **Risk:** **Competitive rhythm** — may fire during the vulnerable T-wave, inducing VF

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** Earliest pacemakers (1950s, designed by Dr. Paul Zoll and Earl Bakken) were asynchronous. They were simple, reliable, and inexpensive but dangerous in patients with intrinsic activity.

### 2.1.2 VVI / AAI — Single-Chamber Demand Mode

- **Paces & Senses:** Either ventricle (VVI) or atrium (AAI)
- **Response:** **Inhibited (I)** — if intrinsic beat is sensed, the pacemaker withholds the next pulse
- **Demand Rate:** Lower rate limit (LRL), typically **60 bpm**
- **Clinical Use:**
    - **AAI:** Sick Sinus Syndrome with intact AV node
    - **VVI:** Chronic AF with slow ventricular response; patients with infrequent pacing needs

### 2.1.3 DDD — Dual-Chamber Universal (AV Sequential) Pacing

- **Paces:** Both atrium AND ventricle
- **Senses:** Both atrium AND ventricle
- **Response:**
    - Atrial event sensed → start AV delay timer (~**150 ms to 200 ms**)
    - If no ventricular event sensed within AV delay → ventricular pace delivered
    - If intrinsic ventricular event sensed → ventricular pace is **inhibited**
- **Advantage:** Preserves AV synchrony, mimics natural cardiac physiology
- **Most physiological mode** for patients with intact sinus node + AV block

### 2.1.4 Rate-Responsive Modes (AAIR, VVIR, DDDR)

- Adds a **sensor** (accelerometer, minute-ventilation sensor, or两者 combined) that detects physical activity and increases pacing rate accordingly
- Upper Tracking Rate (UTR): typically **120 bpm to 130 bpm**
- Critical for **chronotropically incompetent** patients (cannot increase HR with exercise)

### 2.1.5 Special Modes: VVT, AAT, DDI, D00

- **VVT/AAT:** Triggered mode — pacing pulse fires immediately when intrinsic event is sensed (used for diagnostic purposes)
- **DDI:** Paces both, senses both, but atrial tracking is disabled (used in atrial tachyarrhythmias to prevent rapid ventricular pacing)
- **D00 (DOO):** Asynchronous AV sequential — used during MRI conditional mode or surgery

## 2.2 External vs. Implantable Pacemakers

### 2.2.1 External (Transcutaneous) Pacemaker

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| Electrodes | Large skin-surface pads (anterior/posterior or sternum/apex) |
| Pulse Amplitude | **20 mA to 200 mA** (much higher than implantable) |
| Pulse Width | **20 ms to 40 ms** |
| Energy per pulse | **25 mJ to 100 mJ** |
| Indications | Emergency, intraoperative, temporary post-op |
| Patient comfort | Painful — requires sedation/analgesia |
| Duration | Hours to days (max) |

### 2.2.2 Implantable (Permanent) Pacemaker

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| Size | ~**45 mm × 50 mm × 7 mm** (about a matchbox) |
| Weight | **17 g to 30 g** |
| Battery | Lithium-iodine (Li/I₂), **2.8 V nominal**, ~**10 years** longevity |
| Lead | Transvenous (endocardial) or epicardial |
| Pulse Amplitude | **0.5 V to 5 V** (programmable) |
| Pulse Width | **0.1 ms to 1.5 ms** |
| Energy per pulse | **25 μJ to 50 μJ** |
| Connector | IS-1 standard (since 1987) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The dramatic difference in pulse energy between external (mJ range) and implantable (μJ range) pacemakers stems from the electrode-tissue interface. External pacemakers must deliver ~**1000× more energy** to penetrate skin, fat, and muscle before reaching the heart.

## 2.3 Pacemaker Standard Codes — The NBG Code (NASPE/BPEG Generic Code)

The **NBG (North American Society of Pacing and Electrophysiology / British Pacing and Electrophysiology Group) code**, established in **1987** and last revised in **2002**, is a **5-position alphanumeric code** that universally describes pacemaker function.

| Position | I | II | III | IV | V |
|----------|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Meaning** | Chamber(s) Paced | Chamber(s) Sensed | Response to Sensing | Rate Modulation | Multisite Pacing |
| **O** | None | None | None | None | None |
| **A** | Atrium | Atrium | — | — | Atrium |
| **V** | Ventricle | Ventricle | — | — | Ventricle |
| **D** | Dual (A+V) | Dual (A+V) | Dual (T+I) | — | Dual (A+V) |
| **T** | — | — | Triggered | — | — |
| **I** | — | — | Inhibited | — | — |
| **R** | — | — | — | Rate Modulation | — |

**Examples:**
- **VVI** → Paces ventricle, senses ventricle, inhibits on sensed beat
- **DDDR** → Dual-chamber paced, dual-chamber sensed, dual response (triggered+inhibited), rate-responsive
- **DDDRO** → CRT-P with rate response but no multisite

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common question asks to decode "VVI" or "DDDR." Remember: Position III can be T (Triggered), I (Inhibited), D (both), R (rate-adaptive is Position IV), or O (None).

## 2.4 Defibrillators — AC vs. DC

### 2.4.1 AC Defibrillator (Obsolete)

- Operates on **50 Hz/60 Hz mains AC** directly
- Current: **~5 A to 6 A** through the chest
- Duration: **0.1 s to 0.5 s**
- **Major Drawback:** Mains AC has a sinusoidal waveform; the current passes through zero **100 times per second** (50 Hz) or **120 times per second** (60 Hz). During zero-crossings, ventricular fibrillation can be re-induced — this is called **"AC-induced refibrillation."**
- Last commercially produced in late 1960s; **replaced by DC defibrillators** by Dr. Bernard Lown's pioneering work (1962).

### 2.4.2 DC Defibrillator (Modern Standard)

- Charges a high-voltage capacitor and discharges it through the patient.
- Energy stored: $E = \dfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ where **C** is typically **32 μF to 50 μF**
- Voltage charged: up to **~3500 V to 5000 V DC**
- Three standard waveforms:
    1. **Monophasic Damped Sinusoid (Lown waveform)** — historical, ~5 ms duration
    2. **Monophasic Truncated Exponential (MTE)** — Edmark waveform
    3. **Biphasic Truncated Exponential (BTE)** — current standard, more effective at lower energy

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units | Typical Values |
|---------|-------------|-------|----------------|
| $E_{\text{stored}} = \dfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ | Energy stored in defibrillator capacitor | Joules (J) | 200, 300, 360 J |
| $E_{\text{delivered}} = E_{\text{stored}} \cdot \dfrac{R_p}{R_p + R_i}$ | Energy actually delivered to patient (resistive divider) | Joules (J) | 70%–85% of stored |
| $\tau = RC$ | Time constant of RC discharge | seconds | 4 ms typical |
| $I(t) = \dfrac{V_0}{R} \cdot e^{-t/RC}$ | Instantaneous current during exponential discharge | Amperes | — |
| $E(t) = E_0 \cdot e^{-2t/RC}$ | Energy remaining in capacitor at time t | Joules | — |
| $T_{\text{pulse}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{pulse}}}{P_{\text{avg}}} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot t_p}{P}$ | Pacemaker pulse energy relation | Joules | 25–50 μJ |
| $E_{\text{pulse}} = V^2 \cdot t_w / R$ | Pacemaker pulse energy (using voltage, pulse width, impedance) | Joules | 25–50 μJ |
| $R_{\text{transthoracic}} = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A}$ | Transthoracic impedance (resistive) | Ohms (Ω) | 25–150 Ω |
| $\eta = \dfrac{R_p}{R_p + R_i}$ | Energy transfer efficiency | dimensionless | 0.70–0.85 |
| $I_{\text{peak}} = \dfrac{V_0}{R_p + R_i}$ | Peak current delivered to patient | Amperes | 30–50 A |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Exam Reminder:** Always distinguish between **stored energy** (set on defibrillator dial) and **delivered energy** (actual energy passing through patient). The delivered energy is always lower due to internal resistance losses.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pacemaker** technology has driven advances in low-power analog IC design, hermetic titanium packaging, and biocompatible lead insulation (silicone, polyurethane).
- **Defibrillator** engineering has contributed to high-voltage capacitor design, insulated-gate bipolar transistor (IGBT) switching circuits, and impedance-compensated waveform shaping.
- Both devices rely on **NATO Stock Numbers, FDA 510(k) clearances, and IEC 60601-1 safety standards** for global medical device compliance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Stored in a Defibrillator Capacitor

A defibrillator's energy-delivery system is fundamentally a **charged capacitor** discharging through a **resistive patient load**. The starting point is the energy stored in a capacitor.

### 3.1.1 Energy Stored in a Capacitor

**Starting Concept:** When a capacitor of capacitance **C** is charged to a potential difference **V**, it stores electrical energy in its electric field.

**Step 1:** The instantaneous power delivered while charging the capacitor from voltage $v$ to $v + dv$ is:

$$
P = v \cdot i = v \cdot C \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dt}
$$

**Step 2:** Energy stored in an infinitesimal voltage increment $dv$:

$$
dE = P \cdot dt = v \cdot C \cdot dv
$$

**Step 3:** Integrate from 0 to the final voltage $V_0$ (the voltage to which the capacitor is charged):

$$
E_{\text{stored}} = \int_{0}^{V_0} C \cdot v \, dv
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integral:

$$
E_{\text{stored}} = C \cdot \left[ \dfrac{v^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{V_0}
$$

**Step 5:** Final expression:

$$
E_{\text{stored}} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V_0^2
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** This energy is held in the capacitor until the switches (typically **SCR** — Silicon Controlled Rectifier, or modern **IGBT** — Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor) close to deliver the shock to the patient.

### 3.1.2 Numerical Verification — Worked Example

**Problem:** A defibrillator uses a $C = 32 \, \mu F$ capacitor charged to $V_0 = 3500 \, V$. Calculate the stored energy.

**Step 1:** Substitute values into the stored energy formula:

$$
E_{\text{stored}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \times (3500)^2
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $V_0^2$:

$$
(3500)^2 = 12{,}250{,}000 = 1.225 \times 10^7 \, V^2
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by capacitance:

$$
C \cdot V_0^2 = 32 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.225 \times 10^7 = 392 \, J
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the ½ factor:

$$
E_{\text{stored}} = \dfrac{392}{2} = 196 \, J \approx 200 \, J
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matches the **200 J** energy setting on most defibrillators — the standard first-shock energy for adult VF termination. The result validates the engineering design.

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Delivered to the Patient (Resistive Voltage Divider Model)

A defibrillator's internal circuit can be modeled as an ideal charged capacitor in series with an **internal resistance** $R_i$ and the **patient (transthoracic) resistance** $R_p$ as the load.

### 3.2.1 Circuit Model

The patient and internal resistance form a **voltage divider** at the instant of discharge.

**Step 1:** Total circuit resistance:

$$
R_{\text{total}} = R_i + R_p
$$

**Step 2:** Voltage across the patient (voltage divider rule):

$$
V_p = V_0 \cdot \dfrac{R_p}{R_i + R_p}
$$

**Step 3:** Energy stored was $E_0 = \dfrac{1}{2}CV_0^2$. The energy delivered to the patient can be derived by considering that the same charge $Q = CV_0$ flows through both $R_i$ and $R_p$, dissipating energy proportionally to resistance.

**Step 4:** Energy dissipated in any resistor in a series RC discharge is:

$$
E_R = \int_0^\infty i^2(t) R \, dt
$$

**Step 5:** For a capacitor discharging through two series resistors, the total energy dissipated equals the stored energy, and the fraction dissipated in $R_p$ is:

$$
\dfrac{E_p}{E_0} = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i + R_p}
$$

**Step 6:** Therefore, energy delivered to patient:

$$
E_{\text{delivered}} = E_{\text{stored}} \cdot \dfrac{R_p}{R_i + R_p} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V_0^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_p}{R_p + R_i}
$$

**Step 7:** Define energy transfer efficiency $\eta$:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{R_p}{R_p + R_i}
$$

**Step 8:** Final compact form:

$$
E_{\text{delivered}} = \eta \cdot E_{\text{stored}}
$$

### 3.2.2 Numerical Example — Delivered Energy Calculation

**Given:**
- $C = 32 \, \mu F$, $V_0 = 3500 \, V$ (200 J stored)
- Internal resistance $R_i = 10 \, \Omega$
- Patient transthoracic resistance $R_p = 50 \, \Omega$

**Step 1:** Compute efficiency:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{50}{50 + 10} = \dfrac{50}{60} = 0.833
$$

**Step 2:** Compute delivered energy:

$$
E_{\text{delivered}} = 0.833 \times 200 = 166.7 \, J
$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation: About **83%** of the stored energy reaches the myocardium — the rest is lost in the internal resistance and connecting leads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Relevance:** Modern biphasic defibrillators use impedance-compensated waveforms that automatically adjust pulse duration based on measured $R_p$, ensuring that the **delivered** energy (not stored) is consistent across patients.

## 3.3 Derivation: Time Constant of the RC Discharge

The capacitor discharges exponentially. The current and voltage waveforms are critical for determining the **defibrillation pulse duration** and **waveform shape**.

### 3.3.1 Differential Equation of RC Discharge

**Step 1:** Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop:

$$
V_C(t) = V_R(t)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute capacitor and resistor relations:

$$
\dfrac{Q(t)}{C} = i(t) \cdot R
$$

**Step 3:** Since current $i = -\dfrac{dQ}{dt}$ (capacitor is discharging):

$$
\dfrac{Q}{C} = -R \cdot \dfrac{dQ}{dt}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to standard first-order ODE form:

$$
\dfrac{dQ}{dt} + \dfrac{1}{RC} Q = 0
$$

**Step 5:** Solve using separation of variables:

$$
\int \dfrac{dQ}{Q} = -\int \dfrac{dt}{RC}
$$

**Step 6:** Integrate:

$$
\ln Q = -\dfrac{t}{RC} + \text{const}
$$

**Step 7:** Apply initial condition $Q(0) = Q_0 = CV_0$:

$$
Q(t) = Q_0 \cdot e^{-t/RC} = CV_0 \cdot e^{-t/RC}
$$

**Step 8:** Voltage across capacitor:

$$
V_C(t) = \dfrac{Q(t)}{C} = V_0 \cdot e^{-t/RC}
$$

**Step 9:** Current through patient (Ohm's law on $R_p$):

$$
I(t) = \dfrac{V_C(t)}{R_p + R_i} = \dfrac{V_0}{R_p + R_i} \cdot e^{-t/RC}
$$

**Step 10:** Define time constant:

$$
\tau = RC = C \cdot (R_p + R_i)
$$

**Step 11:** Peak current (at $t = 0$):

$$
I_{\text{peak}} = \dfrac{V_0}{R_p + R_i}
$$

### 3.3.2 Numerical Example — Time Constant and Peak Current

**Given:** $C = 32 \, \mu F$, $V_0 = 3500 \, V$, $R_p + R_i = 60 \, \Omega$

**Step 1:** Time constant:

$$
\tau = RC = 32 \times 10^{-6} \times 60 = 1.92 \times 10^{-3} \, s = 1.92 \, ms
$$

**Step 2:** Peak current:

$$
I_{\text{peak}} = \dfrac{3500}{60} = 58.3 \, A
$$

**Step 3:** After $5\tau$ (~9.6 ms), the pulse is effectively over (>99% discharged).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The defibrillation pulse width is therefore typically truncated at **4 ms to 6 ms** for monophasic, and **4 ms to 8 ms** per phase for biphasic. This is much shorter than the absolute refractory period of cardiac muscle (~**200 ms to 300 ms**), preventing re-induction of fibrillation.

## 3.4 Pacemaker Pulse Energy Derivation

The energy delivered by a single pacemaker pulse is much smaller. It is given by:

$$
E_{\text{pulse}} = V^2 \cdot \dfrac{t_w}{R}
$$

where **V** is the pulse amplitude, $t_w$ is the pulse width, and **R** is the lead-myocardial impedance (~**500 Ω to 1000 Ω**).

### 3.4.1 Numerical Example — Pacemaker Pulse Energy

**Given:** $V = 2.5 \, V$, $t_w = 0.5 \, ms = 5 \times 10^{-4} \, s$, $R = 500 \, \Omega$

**Step 1:** Compute $V^2$:

$$
V^2 = (2.5)^2 = 6.25 \, V^2
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into formula:

$$
E_{\text{pulse}} = \dfrac{6.25 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{500}
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$
E_{\text{pulse}} = \dfrac{3.125 \times 10^{-3}}{500} = 6.25 \times 10^{-6} \, J = 6.25 \, \mu J
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: A typical pacemaker pulse delivers only **~6 μJ** — this is enough to depolarize the myocardium (threshold: ~**0.5 V to 1.0 V** at 0.5 ms) but not enough to cause skeletal muscle contraction or pain.

## 3.5 KTU Analytical Matrix — Comparison of AC vs. DC Defibrillator

| Parameter | AC Defibrillator | DC Defibrillator |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| **Power Source** | Mains AC (50/60 Hz) | Charged Capacitor (DC) |
| **Waveform** | Sinusoidal | Damped sinusoid / Truncated exponential / Biphasic |
| **Peak Voltage** | ~**250 V RMS** (mains-derived) | **1000 V – 5000 V DC** |
| **Peak Current** | **5 A – 6 A** | **30 A – 50 A** |
| **Pulse Duration** | **100 ms – 500 ms** | **4 ms – 12 ms** |
| **Refibrillation Risk** | High (zero-crossings re-trigger VF) | Low (monophasic); Very Low (biphasic) |
| **Energy Control** | None (fixed mains) | Variable, selectable (2 J – 360 J) |
| **Synchronization** | Difficult | Easy (R-wave sync for cardioversion) |
| **Portability** | None (mains-locked) | High (battery-powered AED) |
| **Myocardial Damage** | Severe (prolonged current) | Minimal (short pulse) |
| **Current Status** | **Obsolete** (1960s) | **Universal standard** (modern) |
| **Inventor** | Kouwenhoven (1933) | Lown (1962) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of an Implantable Pacemaker (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Bat["LITHIUM IODINE BATTERY 2.8V 10YR"] --> Reg["VOLTAGE REGULATOR AND DC DC CONVERTER"]
    Reg --> Micro["MICROCONTROLLER AND LOGIC CIRCUIT"]
    Reg --> CapChg["CHARGING CIRCUIT FOR OUTPUT CAPACITOR"]
    Micro --> OutSw["OUTPUT SWITCH IGBT OR MOSFET"]
    CapChg --> OutSw
    OutSw --> Lead["PACING LEAD TO MYOCARDIUM"]
    Micro --> SensAmp["SENSE AMPLIFIER FOR INTRINSIC ECG"]
    SensAmp --> Micro
    Micro --> Telemetry["TELEMETRY COIL FOR PROGRAMMER COMMUNICATION"]
    Micro --> RateSens["RATE ADAPTIVE SENSOR ACCELEROMETER"]
    RateSens --> Micro

    classDef power fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A0522D,color:#000000
    classDef logic fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    classDef sensor fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000

    class Bat,Reg,CapChg power
    class Micro,OutSw logic
    class Lead output
    class SensAmp,Telemetry,RateSens sensor
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Each block represents a major functional subsystem. The **battery** powers everything; the **microcontroller** processes sensed signals and decides whether to deliver a pacing pulse; the **output switch** is the high-current pathway that delivers the pulse to the **pacing lead**; the **sense amplifier** detects intrinsic R-waves; the **telemetry coil** allows non-invasive reprogramming via an external programmer.

## 4.2 Block Diagram of a DC Defibrillator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Mains["AC MAINS OR BATTERY 12V"] --> HVInv["HIGH VOLTAGE INVERTER HV DC DC"]
    HVInv --> Rect["HV RECTIFIER AND CHARGE CONTROLLER"]
    Rect --> HVCap["HIGH VOLTAGE CAPACITOR 32 UF 5000V"]
    HVCap --> DischSw["DISCHARGE SWITCH SCR OR IGBT"]
    DischSw --> Pads["DEFIBRILLATION PADS OR PADDLES"]
    Pads --> Patient["PATIENT THORACIC IMPEDANCE 25 TO 150 OHM"]

    Mains --> UI["USER INTERFACE AND ENERGY DIAL"]
    UI --> Ctrl["MICROCONTROLLER"]
    Ctrl --> HVInv
    Ctrl --> DischSw

    Ctrl --> Sync["R WAVE SYNCHRONIZER FOR CARDIOVERSION"]
    Sync --> Ctrl

    classDef hv fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef ctrl fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    classDef user fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000000

    class Mains,HVInv,Rect,HVCap,DischSw,Pads,Patient hv
    class UI,Ctrl,Sync ctrl
    class Sync user
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Functional Notes:**
> - The **R-wave synchronizer** is essential for *cardioversion* (synchronized shock) but must be **disabled** for *defibrillation* (unsynchronized shock) of VF (where there is no R-wave to detect).
> - Modern AEDs (Automated External Defibrillators) integrate the **controller** with impedance measurement and automated rhythm analysis algorithms.

## 4.3 Decision Flow: When to Use Pacemaker vs. Defibrillator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Patient Presents with Arrhythmia"] --> EvalHR{"Heart Rate Pattern?"}

    EvalHR -->|"Bradycardia HR Under 60"| PaceNeed["PACEMAKER INDICATED"]
    PaceNeed --> PaceType{"Chamber Involved?"}
    PaceType -->|"Single Chamber"| SC["AAI or VVI"]
    PaceType -->|"Dual Chamber"| DC["DDD or DDDR"]
    PaceType -->|"CRT BiV"| CRT["Biventricular Pacing"]

    EvalHR -->|"Tachyarrhythmia VF or VT"| DefibNeed["DEFIBRILLATOR INDICATED"]
    DefibNeed --> SyncCheck{"QRS Present and Organized?"}
    SyncCheck -->|"Yes Cardioversion"| SyncShock["Synchronized Shock 50J to 200J"]
    SyncCheck -->|"No VF Unsynchronized"| UnSyncShock["Unsynchronized Shock 200J to 360J"]

    classDef good fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    classDef urgent fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000000

    class Start,PaceNeed,DefibNeed good
    class SyncShock,UnSyncShock urgent
    class EvalHR,PaceType,SyncCheck decision
```

## 4.4 Pacemaker Lead Placement Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SVC["SUPERIOR VENA CAVA"] --> RA["RIGHT ATRIUM"]
    RA --> RV["RIGHT VENTRICLE"]
    RA --> CS["CORONARY SINUS LV LEAD FOR CRT"]
    CS --> LV["LEFT VENTRICLE EPICARDIAL"]
    RV --> Apex["RV APEX PACING SITE"]

    PulseGen["PULSE GENERATOR IMPLANTED IN LEFT OR RIGHT PECTORAL POCKET"] --> RV
    PulseGen --> RA
    PulseGen --> CS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** Modern pacing leads are **bipolar** (two conductors within a single catheter) to reduce interference and provide better signal-to-noise ratio for sensing, compared to older **unipolar** leads (using the pulse generator can as the anode).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Define a cardiac pacemaker. List any three clinical indications where pacemaker implantation becomes necessary.**

**Model Answer:**

A **cardiac pacemaker** is an electronic, battery-powered medical device that delivers low-energy electrical pulses to the heart muscle to initiate and maintain an adequate heart rate when the intrinsic conduction system fails.

**Three clinical indications:**
1. **Sick Sinus Syndrome (SSS):** SA node fires too slowly or irregularly.
2. **Complete (Third-Degree) AV Block:** Electrical signals from atria cannot reach ventricles; pulse generator restores AV synchrony.
3. **Symptomatic Bradycardia:** Sustained HR < 40 bpm causing syncope, fatigue, or hemodynamic compromise.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 per indication)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the NBG pacemaker code "DDDR." What does each letter signify?**

**Model Answer:**

The **NBG code** is a 5-position universal code for describing pacemaker function. Decoding **DDDR**:

| Position | Letter | Meaning |
|----------|--------|---------|
| I (Chamber Paced) | **D** | Dual — both Atrium and Ventricle paced |
| II (Chamber Sensed) | **D** | Dual — both Atrium and Ventricle sensed |
| III (Response to Sensing) | **D** | Dual — Triggered (on atrial event) + Inhibited (on ventricular event) |
| IV (Rate Modulation) | **R** | Rate-responsive (sensor-driven rate increase during activity) |
| V (Multisite Pacing) | **O** | None |

**Clinical Interpretation:** A DDDR pacemaker provides **AV sequential pacing** with **rate adaptation** during physical activity. It is the most physiological pacing mode for active patients with combined SA node disease and AV block.

*(3 marks: 1 for code structure + 1 for full decode + 1 for clinical relevance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional subsystems of an **implantable cardiac pacemaker**. Discuss the role of the lithium-iodine battery and pacing lead in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **external and implantable pacemakers** in terms of pulse amplitude, pulse width, energy per pulse, electrode type, and clinical duration of use. Why is the energy of an external pacemaker ~**1000× greater** than an implantable one? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A modern implantable pacemaker is a **hermetically sealed titanium can** (~17–30 g) containing seven functional subsystems:

1. **Power Source — Lithium-Iodine (Li/I₂) Battery:**
    - Nominal voltage: **2.8 V**
    - Capacity: **0.8 Ah to 2.5 Ah**
    - Longevity: **7 to 12 years** (depends on pacing burden)
    - **Self-discharge rate: < 1% per year** (superior to Li-ion, Li-CFx)
    - **Solid-state chemistry** — no gas generation, safe for hermetic sealing
    - Internal resistance increases gradually, providing a **natural elective replacement indicator (ERI)** ~**2.5 V**

2. **DC-DC Converter / Voltage Multiplier:**
    - Boosts 2.8 V battery to programmable pacing output (**0.5 V to 7.5 V**)
    - Uses **charge pump** or **flyback topology**

3. **Output Stage (Switching Circuit):**
    - **MOSFET/CMOS switches** deliver the pacing pulse to the lead
    - Pulse width: **0.05 ms to 1.5 ms** (programmable)
    - Modern devices use **autocapture algorithms** that adjust output to **2× the capture threshold** to conserve battery

4. **Sense Amplifier:**
    - Detects intrinsic R-waves (ventricular) or P-waves (atrial)
    - Bandwidth: **30 Hz to 200 Hz** (filtered to reject muscle noise)
    - Sensitivity: **0.5 mV to 5 mV** (programmable)

5. **Microcontroller / Logic Circuit:**
    - Implements the pacing algorithm (VVI, DDD, etc.)
    - Manages timing, refractory periods, and AV delay
    - Stores diagnostic data (e.g., percentage of paced beats, AT/AF burden)

6. **Telemetry Coil:**
    - Enables **MICS (Medical Implant Communication Service)** band communication at **402–405 MHz**
    - Allows non-invasive reprogramming and data download

7. **Pacing Lead:**
    - **Transvenous** (endocardial) or **epicardial**
    - **Bipolar** (preferred) with two conductors separated by insulation
    - Tip electrode: platinum-iridium alloy
    - Steroid-eluting (e.g., dexamethasone) tip to reduce inflammation and lower chronic threshold

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Parameter | External Pacemaker | Implantable Pacemaker |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Pulse Amplitude | **20 mA – 200 mA** | **0.5 V – 5 V** (≈ **1 mA – 15 mA**) |
| Pulse Width | **20 ms – 40 ms** | **0.1 ms – 1.5 ms** |
| Energy per Pulse | **25 mJ – 100 mJ** | **25 μJ – 50 μJ** |
| Electrode Type | Skin-surface adhesive pads | Transvenous endocardial lead |
| Tissue Path | Skin → fat → muscle → heart | Direct contact with endocardium |
| Battery | Mains AC or 9V battery | Lithium-iodine (sealed) |
| Duration | Hours to weeks | 7–12 years |
| Patient Comfort | Painful (requires sedation) | Imperceptible |

**Why is the external energy ~1000× greater?**

The energy must traverse **multiple high-impedance biological layers** — stratum corneum (~**100 kΩ·cm²**), subcutaneous fat, skeletal muscle, pericardium, and finally the myocardium. The combined impedance is **500 Ω to 2000 Ω** vs. **300 Ω to 1000 Ω** for a direct endocardial lead. By Ohm's law $P = V^2/R$, to overcome this and reach the heart, the external device must deliver much higher current (mA range, vs. μA for implantable). Also, the **current density** at the skin must be sufficient to penetrate without prolonged exposure — hence the larger pulse widths (~**20–40 ms** vs. ~**0.5 ms**).

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**(a)** Describe the construction and working of a **DC defibrillator** with a block diagram. Explain the significance of the Lown waveform and biphasic truncated exponential waveform in defibrillation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for energy stored in a defibrillator capacitor. A **32 μF** capacitor in a defibrillator is charged to **4000 V**. Calculate (i) the stored energy, (ii) the energy delivered to a patient with transthoracic impedance of **70 Ω** if the internal resistance of the defibrillator is **10 Ω**, and (iii) the peak current. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction of a DC Defibrillator:**

A DC defibrillator consists of:
1. **High-voltage step-up transformer** (mains 230 V → ~2000–3500 V AC)
2. **HV rectifier** (full-wave bridge of high-voltage diodes)
3. **Energy storage capacitor** (32–50 μF, oil-filled, rated ~5 kV)
4. **Charge indicator** (voltmeter or LED bar graph)
5. **Discharge switch** (SCR, IGBT, or thyratron) — triggered by clinician
6. **Inductor** (in Lown waveform) for shaping the damped sinusoidal pulse
7. **Paddles / adhesive pads** (electrode interface)
8. **R-wave synchronizer** (for synchronized cardioversion)
9. **ECG monitor** (for rhythm analysis)

**Working Principle:**
- AC mains → step-up transformer → HV rectifier → charges capacitor to set energy level.
- When clinician presses discharge buttons, the SCR/IGBT fires.
- Capacitor discharges through patient (or through Lown inductor + patient in series).
- Energy is delivered in a brief pulse (4–12 ms).

**Lown Waveform (Monophasic Damped Sinusoid):**
- Designed by **Dr. Bernard Lown (1962)**.
- Contains an **inductor (L)** in series with the patient, forming an **underdamped RLC circuit**.
- The current oscillates and decays sinusoidally — typically **2 to 3 cycles** of decreasing amplitude.
- Pulse width: **5 ms to 8 ms**.
- **Disadvantage:** Higher peak current → greater myocardial injury at high energies.

**Biphasic Truncated Exponential (BTE) Waveform:**
- Current modern standard.
- Phase 1: positive current pulse for ~**4 ms**, then truncated.
- Phase 2: reversed polarity current pulse for ~**4 ms**.
- Total pulse: **~8 ms**.
- **Advantages:**
    - Lower energy requirement (typically **150 J to 200 J** vs. **200 J to 360 J** for monophasic)
    - Higher first-shock success rate (>90% for VF)
    - Less myocardial damage
    - Automatic impedance compensation adjusts pulse width

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation of Stored Energy:**

The energy $E$ delivered to charge a capacitor from 0 to voltage $V$ is given by integrating the instantaneous power $P = v \cdot i$ over the charging process. Since $i = C \cdot dv/dt$:

$$
E = \int_0^V v \cdot C \, dv = \dfrac{1}{2}CV^2
$$

**Given:** $C = 32 \, \mu F = 32 \times 10^{-6} \, F$, $V = 4000 \, V$, $R_p = 70 \, \Omega$, $R_i = 10 \, \Omega$

**(i) Stored Energy:**

$$
E_{\text{stored}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \times (4000)^2
$$

Step 1: $V^2 = 1.6 \times 10^7 \, V^2$

Step 2: $C \cdot V^2 = 32 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.6 \times 10^7 = 512 \, J$

Step 3: $E_{\text{stored}} = \dfrac{512}{2} = 256 \, J$

**(ii) Energy Delivered to Patient:**

Step 1: Compute efficiency $\eta$:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{R_p}{R_p + R_i} = \dfrac{70}{70 + 10} = \dfrac{70}{80} = 0.875
$$

Step 2: Compute delivered energy:

$$
E_{\text{delivered}} = \eta \cdot E_{\text{stored}} = 0.875 \times 256 = 224 \, J
$$

**(iii) Peak Current:**

Step 1: Total resistance $R_{\text{total}} = 70 + 10 = 80 \, \Omega$

Step 2: Peak current at $t = 0$:

$$
I_{\text{peak}} = \dfrac{V_0}{R_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{4000}{80} = 50 \, A
$$

**Summary of Results:**
- $E_{\text{stored}} = 256 \, J$
- $E_{\text{delivered}} = 224 \, J$
- $I_{\text{peak}} = 50 \, A$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Computing delivered energy using the **square of the voltage divider ratio** $\left(V_0 \cdot \dfrac{R_p}{R_p+R_i}\right)^2 \cdot \dfrac{C}{2}$ — this is **incorrect**. The correct formula is $E_{\text{stored}} \cdot \dfrac{R_p}{R_p+R_i}$, NOT $E_{\text{stored}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{R_p}{R_p+R_i}\right)^2$. This distinction is often tested.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **peak current** with **average current** or **RMS current**. The peak occurs at $t = 0$ of the discharge, not at the midpoint.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Writing $E = CV^2$ instead of $E = \dfrac{1}{2}CV^2$. The factor of ½ is **mandatory**.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting to convert $\mu F$ to $F$ in the calculation. $32 \, \mu F = 32 \times 10^{-6} \, F$, not $32 \, F$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU):**

- **Pacemaker** = low-energy (μJ), initiates missing beats (bradycardia).
- **Defibrillator** = high-energy (J), terminates lethal tachyarrhythmias (VF/VT).
- **NBG Code** is 5-position: I (Paced chamber) — II (Sensed chamber) — III (Response: T/I/D) — IV (Rate Modulation: R) — V (Multisite: A/V/D).
- **AAI** → atrial demand; **VVI** → ventricular demand; **DDD** → AV sequential (most physiological).
- **Demand mode** inhibits on sensed beat; **asynchronous mode** (AOO/VOO/DOO) fires at fixed rate regardless.
- **Lithium-Iodine (Li/I₂) battery**: 2.8 V, ~10 years, low self-discharge.
- **External pacemaker pulse**: 20–200 mA, 20–40 ms, mJ range (painful).
- **Implantable pulse**: 0.5–5 V, 0.1–1.5 ms, μJ range.
- **Pacemaker energy formula**: $E_{\text{pulse}} = V^2 t_w / R$
- **AC defibrillator** = obsolete (refibrillation at zero-crossings).
- **DC defibrillator** = charged capacitor discharge, modern standard.
- **Lown waveform** = monophasic damped sinusoid (historical).
- **Biphasic truncated exponential (BTE)** = current gold standard.
- **Capacitor energy**: $E_{\text{stored}} = \dfrac{1}{2}CV^2$
- **Delivered energy**: $E_{\text{delivered}} = E_{\text{stored}} \cdot \dfrac{R_p}{R_p + R_i}$ (linear resistive ratio, NOT squared).
- **Time constant**: $\tau = R \cdot C$; pulse truncated at 4–5τ.
- **Peak current**: $I_{\text{peak}} = V_0 / (R_p + R_i)$.
- **Standard adult defibrillation energy**: 200 J → 300 J → 360 J (escalating).
- **Pediatric dose**: 2 J/kg initially, then 4 J/kg.
- **R-wave synchronization** = required for cardioversion; **disabled** for VF defibrillation.
- **Impedance range**: Adult transthoracic 25–150 Ω; higher impedance = lower delivered energy.
- **Battery longevity indicator**:ERI ~2.5 V; EOL ~2.0 V; both trigger device alerts.
- **MICS band** (402–405 MHz) = telemetry frequency for implantable programmer communication.
- **Capture threshold** = minimum pulse required to depolarize myocardium; modern devices autocapture at 2× threshold.
- **Hysteresis**: programmable feature allowing HR to drop to ~50 bpm before pacing resumes (preserves intrinsic rhythm).
- **PVARP** (Post-Ventricular Atrial Refractory Period): ~300 ms; prevents pacemaker-mediated tachycardia (PMT) by ignoring retrograde P-waves.
- **BiV pacing / CRT**: For LBBB and EF <35% — resynchronizes ventricular contraction via LV lead in coronary sinus.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for defibrillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT741 - THERAPEUTIC EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Need for defibrillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **defibrillator** is a life-saving electronic therapeutic medical device designed to deliver a controlled, high-energy electric shock to the heart through electrode paddles or pads placed on the patient's chest. This shock is termed **defibrillation**, and its primary therapeutic purpose is to terminate life-threatening **cardiac arrhythmias**, specifically **ventricular fibrillation (VF)** and **pulseless ventricular tachycardia (pVT)**, by simultaneously depolarizing a critical mass of myocardial (heart muscle) cells.

The fundamental clinical need arises from the fact that during VF or pVT, the heart's electrical activity becomes chaotic and uncoordinated, causing it to "quiver" rather than pump blood effectively. Without immediate intervention, cerebral ischemia sets in within **4–6 minutes**, leading to irreversible brain damage and death. The defibrillator essentially acts as an **external electrophysiological reset button** that re-establishes a normal sinus rhythm by allowing the heart's natural pacemaker (the sinoatrial node) to resume control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The defibrillator is classified as an **Electrotherapeutic Device** under the broader category of cardiac care equipment. It is a non-pharmacological, energy-based intervention, distinguishing it from drug therapy (antiarrhythmics) or mechanical therapy (CPR).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large orchestra where every musician suddenly starts playing a different tune at a different tempo, each one louder than the next. The result is unbearable noise — no recognizable melody, no rhythm, no harmony. The conductor (the heart's natural pacemaker) is shouting instructions, but no one is listening because the chaos is too overwhelming.

A defibrillator is like the **orchestra manager who suddenly fires a loud cannon**. For a split second, every musician is stunned into silence. In that momentary silence, the conductor's voice is finally heard. When the musicians recover, they instinctively follow the conductor again, restoring a coordinated, beautiful performance (a regular heartbeat).

> [!NOTE]
> The "cannon" is the **truncated exponential or damped sinusoidal biphasic shock** — brief, intense, and designed to stun without permanently damaging the heart muscle.

## 1.3 Why It Is Clinically Essential

The need for a defibrillator is grounded in three clinical realities:

1. **Sudden Cardiac Arrest (SCA) is a leading cause of death globally.** According to the WHO, cardiovascular diseases account for approximately **17.9 million deaths per year**, with SCA being a major contributor.
2. **Survival is time-dependent.** For every minute that defibrillation is delayed, the survival rate drops by **7–10%**. After 10 minutes without defibrillation, survival is rare.
3. **Pharmacological therapy is too slow.** Drugs like amiodarone or epinephrine take minutes to circulate, whereas electrical shock is instantaneous.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The goal of defibrillation is to deliver sufficient **peak current (amperes)** through the myocardium to depolarize a critical mass of cells — typically requiring **30–50 joules per kilogram of body weight** in biphasic adult defibrillators.

## 1.4 Key Terms for KTU Board Examinations

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Cardiac Arrhythmia** | Any abnormality in the rate, rhythm, or conduction of the heartbeat. |
| **Ventricular Fibrillation (VF)** | Rapid, chaotic, and ineffective contractions of the ventricles; no cardiac output. |
| **Pulseless VT (pVT)** | Very rapid ventricular rhythm that produces no effective pulse. |
| **Synchronized Cardioversion** | A timed shock delivered to coincide with the R-wave of the QRS complex. |
| **Unsynchronized Defibrillation** | An immediate, untimed shock used in emergencies where there is no R-wave to sync with. |
| **Automated External Defibrillator (AED)** | A portable, computer-controlled defibrillator usable by laypersons. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrophysiological Basis of the Need

To understand *why* a defibrillator is needed, we must first understand *how* the heart's electrical system fails.

### 2.1.1 Normal Cardiac Conduction

Under normal physiology, the heart is paced by the **Sinoatrial (SA) node** located in the right atrium. The SA node generates an action potential at a rate of **60–100 beats per minute (bpm)**. This depolarization wave spreads through the atria, reaches the **Atrioventricular (AV) node**, is delayed briefly (to allow ventricular filling), and then propagates through the **Bundle of His**, **bundle branches**, and **Purkinje fibers** to depolarize the ventricles.

This produces the characteristic **P-QRS-T waveform** on an ECG.

### 2.1.2 Pathophysiology of Fibrillation

During VF, multiple **re-entrant wavelets** propagate simultaneously across the ventricular myocardium. These wavelets collide, extinguish, and regenerate in a self-sustaining, chaotic pattern. The result is:

- **No synchronized ventricular contraction** → no stroke volume → no cardiac output.
- **Cerebral blood flow = 0** → loss of consciousness within 10 seconds.
- **Myocardial oxygen depletion** → irreversible injury if not corrected within minutes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Therapeutic Goal:** Deliver an electric field of sufficient magnitude (typically **5–10 V/cm** across the myocardium) to simultaneously depolarize all excitable myocardial tissue. This terminates the re-entrant circuits, creating a brief **refractory period** during which the SA node can reclaim dominance.

## 2.2 Classification of Defibrillators (Based on Clinical Need)

| Defibrillator Type | Clinical Setting | Key Need Addressed |
|-------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Manual External** | Hospitals, ambulances | Physician-controlled energy selection |
| **Automated External (AED)** | Public spaces, airports | Layperson use, voice prompts |
| **Implantable (ICD)** | High-risk patients | Continuous monitoring, automatic shock |
| **Wearable Cardioverter Defibrillator (WCD)** | Temporary risk patients | Non-invasive 24/7 protection |
| **Biphasic** | Modern standard | Lower energy, higher efficacy |

## 2.3 Why Biphasic is Preferred Over Monophasic

Modern KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **biphasic waveform defibrillators** as the clinical standard. The need for biphasic technology arises from:

- **Lower energy requirement** (typically **120–200 J** vs. **200–360 J** for monophasic) → less myocardial injury.
- **Higher first-shock success rate** (>90% vs. ~60% for monophasic).
- **Less skin burns** at the electrode site.
- **Better impedance compensation.**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield items. Master the relationships, not just the symbols.

| Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| $E = \frac{V^2}{R} \cdot t$ | Energy delivered, where V is voltage, R is transthoracic impedance, t is duration | Joules (J) |
| $I = \frac{V}{R}$ | Peak current through the chest | Amperes (A) |
| $E = I^2 R t$ | Energy in terms of current (Ohmic heating equivalent) | Joules (J) |
| $\tau = RC$ | RC time constant of the discharge circuit | Seconds (s) |
| $E_{biphasic} \approx 0.6 \times E_{monophasic}$ | Typical energy reduction factor for biphasic | Dimensionless |
| $E_{delivered} = \int_0^t v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt$ | True energy delivered as integral of voltage and current | Joules (J) |
| $P = \frac{E}{t}$ | Instantaneous power during shock | Watts (W) |
| Transthoracic Impedance | Adult: typically **25–150 Ω** | Ohms (Ω) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Never confuse **stored energy** (in the capacitor) with **delivered energy** (to the patient). Delivered energy is always less due to internal losses and patient impedance. KTU exam questions often test this distinction.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The need for defibrillators drives innovation in:

1. **Capacitor technology** — high-energy-density capacitors (e.g., **200 µF at 2000 V** for 400 J storage).
2. **High-voltage switching** — Insulated-Gate Bipolar Transistor (**IGBT**) and Silicon Controlled Rectifier (**SCR**) circuits.
3. **Impedance measurement** — real-time patient impedance sensing for adaptive energy delivery.
4. **ECG analysis algorithms** — for AEDs, the device must distinguish shockable from non-shockable rhythms using **signal processing** (e.g., FFT, wavelet transforms, machine learning).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Stored vs. Energy Delivered

This derivation is a **KTU favorite** — examiners frequently test students on the relationship between capacitor-stored energy and patient-delivered energy.

### Step 1: Energy Stored in the Defibrillator Capacitor

A defibrillator stores energy in a high-voltage capacitor. The energy stored in a capacitor is given by the fundamental electrostatic formula:

$$
E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2
$$

**Logic:** Energy is work done to charge the capacitor to voltage $V$. For a typical adult defibrillator, $C = 32 \, \mu F$ and $V_{max} = 2000 \, V$.

### Step 2: Energy Delivered to the Patient

When the capacitor discharges through the patient's transthoracic impedance $R$, the discharge is governed by an **RC circuit**. The voltage across the patient decays exponentially:

$$
V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/RC}
$$

The current through the patient is:

$$
i(t) = \frac{V(t)}{R} = \frac{V_0}{R} e^{-t/RC}
$$

### Step 3: Instantaneous Power Dissipated in the Patient

Power dissipated in the patient's thoracic resistance is:

$$
P(t) = V(t) \cdot i(t) = \frac{V_0^2}{R} e^{-2t/RC}
$$

### Step 4: Total Energy Delivered (Integration)

Total energy delivered is the time integral of instantaneous power from $t=0$ to $t=\infty$:

$$
E_{delivered} = \int_0^{\infty} P(t) \, dt
$$

Substituting:

$$
E_{delivered} = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{V_0^2}{R} e^{-2t/RC} \, dt
$$

### Step 5: Evaluating the Integral

Pulling out the constants:

$$
E_{delivered} = \frac{V_0^2}{R} \int_0^{\infty} e^{-2t/RC} \, dt
$$

The integral of an exponential decay $\int_0^{\infty} e^{-at} \, dt = \frac{1}{a}$ where $a = \frac{2}{RC}$:

$$
\int_0^{\infty} e^{-2t/RC} \, dt = \frac{RC}{2}
$$

### Step 6: Final Result

$$
E_{delivered} = \frac{V_0^2}{R} \cdot \frac{RC}{2} = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2
$$

**Critical Insight:** The energy delivered to the patient equals the energy originally stored in the capacitor, **provided the discharge is complete (t → ∞)** and there are no internal losses. In practice, truncation at 4–10 ms and internal circuit resistance reduce delivered energy by **10–25%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters clinically:** This is the theoretical foundation for **impedance compensation**. If $R$ is high (e.g., obese patient), the waveform must be extended in time to ensure adequate energy is delivered before the patient is "shocked out" of the rhythm.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Clinical Need vs. Device Response

| Clinical Condition | Heart Rate | Cardiac Output | Patient Conscious? | Defibrillator Action |
|--------------------|------------|----------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Normal Sinus Rhythm | 60–100 bpm | Normal | Yes | **None** — no need |
| Atrial Fibrillation | 350–600 bpm (atrial) | Reduced | Often yes | **Synchronized Cardioversion** |
| Ventricular Tachycardia (stable) | 100–250 bpm | Reduced | Yes | **Synchronized Cardioversion** |
| Pulseless VT | >250 bpm | **Zero** | No | **Unsynchronized Defibrillation** |
| Ventricular Fibrillation | 400–600 bpm (chaotic) | **Zero** | No | **Unsynchronized Defibrillation** |
| Asystole | 0 bpm | **Zero** | No | **Defibrillation NOT indicated** |
| Pulseless Electrical Activity (PEA) | Any (organized on ECG) | **Zero** | No | **Defibrillation NOT indicated** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Board Exam Point:** Defibrillation is **NEVER** indicated for asystole or PEA. Delivering a shock in these cases is clinically harmful and is a classic exam trap. The mnemonic: **"No QRS — No Shock."**

## 3.3 Derivation: Transthoracic Impedance and Energy Delivery

Transthoracic impedance (TTI) is the resistance the body offers to current flow. Energy delivered at a given voltage depends on TTI:

$$
E_{delivered} = \frac{V^2}{R_{TTI}} \cdot t_{eff}
$$

Where $t_{eff}$ is the effective pulse duration. For a monophasic truncated exponential:

$$
t_{eff} \approx \frac{R_{TTI} \cdot C}{2} \cdot (1 - e^{-2t_{trunc}/R_{TTI} C})
$$

**Implication:** If $R_{TTI} = 50 \, \Omega$ (typical adult) and $C = 32 \, \mu F$, then $\tau = RC = 1.6 \, ms$. A 4 ms pulse delivers approximately:

$$
E_{delivered} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 (1 - e^{-2(4)/1.6}) = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 (1 - e^{-5}) \approx 0.993 \cdot \frac{1}{2} C V^2
$$

This confirms ~99% of stored energy is delivered in a 4 ms pulse at typical adult impedance.

## 3.4 KTU 2024 Analytical Framework: The "Need Hierarchy"

The clinical need for a defibrillator operates at multiple levels:

1. **Cellular level:** Restore transmembrane ionic gradients (Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase reset).
2. **Tissue level:** Depolarize a critical myocardial mass (**>75%** of ventricular mass).
3. **Organ level:** Re-establish coordinated ventricular contraction.
4. **Systemic level:** Restore cerebral and coronary perfusion.
5. **Population level:** Reduce out-of-hospital cardiac arrest mortality.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Defibrillator Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the interaction between the major subsystems of a modern biphasic defibrillator, illustrating the engineering response to the clinical need.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DCPS[DC Power Supply 2000V DC] --> CHG[Charging Circuit and High Voltage Inverter]
    CHG --> CAP[Energy Storage Capacitor C equals 32 uF]
    CAP --> SW[High Voltage Switch IGBT or SCR]
    SW --> TRUNC[Waveform Truncation Control]
    TRUNC --> PT1[Patient Electrode Pad 1]
    TRUNC --> PT2[Patient Electrode Pad 2]
    PT1 --> CHEST[Patient Thoracic Impedance 25 to 150 ohm]
    CHEST --> PT2
    ECG[ECG Acquisition Module] --> DSP[Digital Signal Processor]
    DSP --> DISP[Display and Rhythm Analysis]
    DSP --> CTRL[Microcontroller Sync and Energy Selection]
    CTRL --> TRUNC
    CTRL --> ALARM[Audio Visual Alarm and Voice Prompts]
    CTRL --> CHG
    BAT[Rechargeable Battery] --> DCPS
    BAT --> ECG
    BAT --> DSP
    BAT --> CTRL
```

## 4.2 Clinical Decision Flow: When is Defibrillation Indicated?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Patient with Suspected Cardiac Arrest] --> CHECK{Responsive and Breathing Normally}
    CHECK -->|Yes| MONITOR[Monitor and Investigate Cause]
    CHECK -->|No| CPR[Initiate CPR Immediately]
    CPR --> AED[Attach AED or Manual Defibrillator]
    AED --> ANALYZE{Rhythm Analysis}
    ANALYZE -->|Shockable VF or pVT| SHOCK[Deliver Unsynchronized Shock]
    ANALYZE -->|Non Shockable Asystole or PEA| CPRONLY[Continue CPR No Shock]
    SHOCK --> RESUME[Resume CPR for 2 minutes]
    RESUME --> ANALYZE
    CPRONLY --> RESUME
    RESUME --> ADV[Advanced Life Support if No ROSC]
    ADV --> ANALYZE
```

## 4.3 Energy Selection Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify Patient Type] --> B{Adult}
    A --> C{Pediatric less than 25 kg}
    B --> D[Monophasic 360 J]
    B --> E[Biphasic 120 to 200 J Manufacturer Specific]
    C --> F[2 to 4 J per kg Initial]
    F --> G[Subsequent 4 to 10 J per kg]
    D --> H[Increase if First Shock Fails]
    E --> H
    G --> H
```

## 4.4 Schematic Annotation Table

| Node ID | Component | Engineering Function |
|---------|-----------|----------------------|
| DCPS | DC Power Supply | Steps up battery voltage to 2000 V DC via flyback or boost converter |
| CHG | Charging Circuit | Controls charging current; receives set-point from microcontroller |
| CAP | Storage Capacitor | Stores energy $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ |
| SW | High-Voltage Switch | Solid-state switch; must handle 2000 V and peak currents of 30–50 A |
| TRUNC | Waveform Truncation | H-bridge configuration to produce biphasic waveform |
| ECG | ECG Module | Patient isolation via optocouplers; bandwidth 0.5–40 Hz |
| DSP | Digital Signal Processor | Runs rhythm detection algorithms (e.g., VF detection sensitivity >90%) |
| CTRL | Microcontroller | Master control; manages user interface, energy selection, synchronization |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q: Define defibrillation. Why is a defibrillator considered an essential life-saving device in emergency cardiac care?**

**Model Answer:**

Defibrillation is the process of delivering a controlled, high-energy electric shock to the heart to terminate life-threatening arrhythmias such as ventricular fibrillation (VF) and pulseless ventricular tachycardia (pVT).

A defibrillator is considered essential because:

1. **Sudden Cardiac Arrest (SCA)** is one of the leading causes of death worldwide, and the majority of SCAs are caused by VF or pVT.
2. The only definitive treatment for these rhythms is **electrical defibrillation** — drugs and CPR alone cannot terminate VF.
3. Survival decreases by **7–10% for every minute** defibrillation is delayed, making portable defibrillators (AEDs) critical in public spaces.

*(3 marks awarded for: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for identifying VF/pVT, 1 mark for time-dependence justification)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**Q: List and briefly explain any three clinical conditions that require the use of a defibrillator.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Ventricular Fibrillation (VF):** The ventricles quiver chaotically at 400–600 bpm with no coordinated contraction. Defibrillation depolarizes the entire myocardium, allowing the SA node to re-establish normal rhythm.

2. **Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia (pVT):** A rapid ventricular rhythm (>250 bpm) that fails to produce effective cardiac output. Defibrillation is required immediately.

3. **Synchronized Cardioversion** (for stable VT with a pulse): A timed shock synchronized with the R-wave of the QRS complex is used to avoid the vulnerable T-wave period, which could otherwise induce VF.

*(3 marks: 1 mark each for correct identification and brief explanation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme guidelines, Part B questions in ESE carry **14 marks** and typically offer an internal choice. Two alternative questions are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the electrophysiological basis of ventricular fibrillation and how a defibrillator terminates it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the functional architecture of a modern biphasic external defibrillator. Discuss why biphasic waveforms are clinically preferred over monophasic waveforms. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Electrophysiological Basis and Defibrillator Action (7 marks):**

The normal heartbeat originates from the **Sinoatrial (SA) node**, which generates a rhythmic action potential that propagates through the atria, AV node, and ventricles via the His-Purkinje system, producing a coordinated contraction.

In **Ventricular Fibrillation (VF)**, this orderly propagation breaks down. Multiple **re-entrant wavelets** form and propagate simultaneously across the ventricular myocardium. These wavelets collide and re-generate, creating a self-sustaining, chaotic electrical state with no synchronized contraction. The ventricles merely "quiver," resulting in **zero cardiac output**, loss of consciousness within 10 seconds, and death within minutes if untreated.

A **defibrillator** delivers a high-energy electric shock (typically 120–360 J) through two electrode pads placed on the patient's chest. The shock creates a strong electric field across the myocardium (5–10 V/cm), which **simultaneously depolarizes the entire mass of ventricular muscle cells**. This terminates all re-entrant circuits by placing the myocardium in a uniform refractory state. After the brief refractory period, the **SA node**, being the fastest natural pacemaker, reasserts control, and a normal sinus rhythm is restored.

**(b) Block Diagram and Biphasic vs. Monophasic (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (as in SECTION 4.1):** The major subsystems include —

1. **DC Power Supply and High-Voltage Inverter** — steps up battery voltage to 2000 V.
2. **Energy Storage Capacitor** — typically 32 µF, stores energy as $\frac{1}{2}CV^2$.
3. **High-Voltage Switch** — IGBT or SCR, controls discharge.
4. **Waveform Shaping and Truncation Circuit** — H-bridge for biphasic output.
5. **Patient Electrode Pads** — deliver current across the thorax.
6. **ECG Acquisition Module** — monitors rhythm and provides sync trigger.
7. **Microcontroller and DSP** — orchestrate charging, energy selection, synchronization, and rhythm analysis.
8. **Display and Audio/Visual Alarms** — for user feedback.

**Biphasic vs. Monophasic Preference:**

| Parameter | Monophasic | Biphasic |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Energy required | 200–360 J | 120–200 J |
| First-shock success | ~60% | >90% |
| Myocardial injury | Higher | Lower |
| Impedance compensation | Limited | Adaptive |
| Post-shock ST changes | More severe | Minimal |

The biphasic waveform first delivers current in one direction, then reverses polarity. This reversal **resets the cell membrane potential** and allows lower total energy to achieve the same defibrillation threshold, reducing myocardial damage.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**Q: (a)** Derive the expression for the energy stored in a defibrillator capacitor and show that the energy delivered to a patient equals the stored energy for a complete RC discharge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the clinical situations where defibrillation is **contraindicated**, and explain why unsynchronized shocks are used in emergency VF rather than synchronized shocks. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation of Stored and Delivered Energy (7 marks):**

*(The full derivation is provided in SECTION 3.1 of these notes. Students should reproduce the following key steps:)*

**Step 1 — Energy stored in a capacitor:**

$$
E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2
$$

**Step 2 — Discharge voltage and current in an RC circuit:**

$$
V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/RC} \qquad i(t) = \frac{V_0}{R} e^{-t/RC}
$$

**Step 3 — Instantaneous power in the patient:**

$$
P(t) = \frac{V_0^2}{R} e^{-2t/RC}
$$

**Step 4 — Total energy delivered (integration):**

$$
E_{delivered} = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{V_0^2}{R} e^{-2t/RC} \, dt
$$

$$
E_{delivered} = \frac{V_0^2}{R} \cdot \frac{RC}{2} = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2
$$

**Conclusion:** For a complete discharge, $E_{delivered} = E_{stored}$. In practice, the waveform is **truncated at 4–10 ms** and internal circuit losses reduce delivered energy by 10–25%.

**Numerical Example:** For $C = 32 \, \mu F$ and $V_0 = 2000 \, V$:

$$
E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \times (2000)^2 = 64 \, J \text{ (at lower settings)}
$$

*(Full marks for: 2 marks for stored energy formula, 3 marks for integration, 2 marks for numerical example.)*

**(b) Contraindications and Unsynchronized Shocks (7 marks):**

**Defibrillation is contraindicated in:**

1. **Asystole (flatline):** There is no electrical activity to "reset." Shocking a flatline is futile and causes myocardial injury.
2. **Pulseless Electrical Activity (PEA):** The ECG may show organized rhythms, but there is no mechanical contraction. The cause is typically mechanical (e.g., tamponade, tension pneumothorax), not electrical.
3. **Conscious patients with a pulse:** In stable rhythms, elective synchronized cardioversion under sedation is preferred.

**Why unsynchronized shock in VF?**

In VF, the ECG shows chaotic, irregular activity with **no identifiable QRS complex or R-wave**. A synchronized shock requires a clear R-wave to time delivery, avoiding the **vulnerable T-wave period** (which corresponds to ventricular repolarization). If a shock lands on the T-wave, it can induce VF — the very rhythm we are trying to treat.

Therefore, in pulseless VF or pVT, there is no R-wave to synchronize with. The defibrillator delivers the shock **immediately and unsynchronized**, accepting the (statistically negligible) risk of T-wave coincidence in exchange for speed — because every second of delay reduces survival by 7–10%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "defibrillation" with "cardioversion":** Cardioversion is *synchronized*; defibrillation is *unsynchronized*. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to state contraindications explicitly:** Many students describe when to shock but fail to state when *not* to shock. KTU examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Skipping the integration step in the derivation:** Writing $\int_0^{\infty} e^{-2t/RC} dt = \frac{RC}{2}$ without showing the substitution $a = \frac{2}{RC}$ loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing "|x|" in markdown tables:** Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX. Plain vertical pipes break the markdown table rendering.
> 5. **Forgetting the numerical example:** KTU 2024 scheme rewards students who substitute realistic values (C = 32 µF, V = 2000 V) to verify their derivation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield KTU Points:**

- [x] A **defibrillator** delivers a high-energy electric shock to terminate **VF** and **pVT**.
- [x] Survival drops **7–10% per minute** without defibrillation.
- [x] Normal heart rate: **60–100 bpm**; VF: **400–600 bpm** chaotic.
- [x] Energy stored: $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ — usually $C = 32 \, \mu F$, $V = 2000 \, V$.
- [x] **Delivered energy = Stored energy** for a complete RC discharge (theoretical).
- [x] **Biphasic** is preferred over monophasic: lower energy, higher success, less injury.
- [x] Transthoracic impedance: typically **25–150 Ω** in adults.
- [x] **Defibrillation is contraindicated in asystole and PEA.**
- [x] **Synchronized** shock = cardioversion (R-wave triggered). **Unsynchronized** = defibrillation (emergency).
- [x] Vulnerable period: the **T-wave** on ECG — never shock on the T-wave (can induce VF).
- [x] Critical myocardial mass to depolarize: **>75%** of ventricles.
- [x] Required electric field across the heart: **5–10 V/cm**.
- [x] Key engineering components: **DC–DC inverter, capacitor, IGBT/SCR switch, H-bridge truncator, ECG isolator, microcontroller.**
- [x] AEDs are designed for layperson use with **voice prompts** and **automated rhythm analysis**.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1
