**Comparison — Rigid vs Flexible (1 table, 2 marks):**

| Feature | Rigid | Flexible |
|---|---|---|
| Optics | HOPKINS rod-lenses | Coherent fibre bundle |
| Image quality | Excellent (high MTF) | Moderate |
| Tip deflection | None | Multi-plane |
| Patient comfort | Low (needs GA + incision) | High (sedation only) |
| Sterilisation | Autoclave-friendly | Chemical (Cidex) |
| Applications | Laparoscopy, cystoscopy | GI endoscopy |

---

### (b) Derivation of NA + Numerical Problem [7 marks]

**Derivation (5 marks):** Provide the full derivation from Section 3.1 (Steps 1–9) of these notes. The final expression:

$$
NA \;=\; \sqrt{n_1^{2} - n_2^{2}}
$$

**Numerical problem (2 marks):**

Given $n_1 = 1.50$, $n_2 = 1.46$.

$$
NA \;=\; \sqrt{(1.50)^{2} - (1.46)^{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{2.25 - 2.1316} \;=\; \sqrt{0.1184} \;\approx\; 0.344
$$

Acceptance angle:

$$
\theta_a \;=\; \sin^{-1}(0.344) \;\approx\; 20.12^\circ
$$

**Interpretation:** Light rays launched within a cone of half-angle $20.12^\circ$ about the fibre axis will be guided by TIR.

---

## 5.5 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in this question:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cold light source** in the block diagram — examiners specifically look for this; failure to include it costs at least **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing NA with refractive index** — they are *dimensionless* but *different quantities*. NA is always $\leq n_1$.
> 3. **Writing "CCD" for a fibrescope** — fibrescopes do *not* have a CCD at the tip; the CCD is in the *external* camera head. Don't confuse it with a video-endoscope.
> 4. **Skipping the insufflator** in a laparoscope diagram — pneumoperitoneum is a defining feature of laparoscopy.
> 5. **Writing the derivation of NA without the Snell's Law steps** — partial marks only. The full sequence (air-core refraction + cladding condition + simplification) must be shown.
> 6. **Forgetting the units** of the acceptance angle (degrees) and NA (dimensionless).
> 7. **Not labelling direction of view** ($0^\circ$ vs $30^\circ$) in a laparoscope diagram.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Endoscope** = general term; **Laparoscope** = rigid endoscope for abdomen/pelvis.
- **Fibre optic endoscope** transmits light and image via **coherent + non-coherent fibre bundles** using **TIR**.
- **Numerical Aperture:** $NA = \sqrt{n_1^{2} - n_2^{2}}$ — measures light-gathering ability.
- **Coherent image bundle** preserves image; **non-coherent** bundles are only for illumination.
- **Critical angle condition:** $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$.
- **Block diagram (must memorise):** Light source → condenser → light guide → illumination bundle → body cavity → objective lens → image bundle → eyepiece → CCD/CMOS camera → video processor → monitor → recorder.
- **HOPKINS rod-lens** system is the gold standard for rigid laparoscopes.
- **Pneumoperitoneum** is created by a **CO$_2$ insufflator** at **12–15 mmHg** — essential for laparoscopy.
- **Cold light source** uses an **IR filter** to deliver heat-free visible light.
- **Direction of view:** $0^\circ$ (forward) or **$30^\circ$ angled** (rotatable).
- **Standard laparoscope diameters:** **5 mm and 10 mm**.
- **Resolution comparison:** Fibrescope = 40k–100k pixels; Video-endoscope = 2–8 megapixels.
- **Numerical values to remember:** NA of medical fibres = **0.2–0.5**; FOV = **$70^\circ$–$120^\circ$**; Wavelength = **400–700 nm**; Insufflation pressure = **12–15 mmHg**.
- **Sterilisation:** Rigid scopes → autoclave; Flexible fibrescopes → chemical immersion (glutaraldehyde / peracetic acid).
- **Modern trend:** Video-endoscopes with **4K resolution, NBI (Narrow Band Imaging)**, and **AI-assisted polyp detection**.
- **Robot-assisted laparoscopy (Da Vinci system)** uses the same fibre-optic + camera principles but adds 3D vision and wristed instruments.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line recall mantra for the exam:**
> *"Light in by non-coherent bundle; image out by coherent bundle; CCD converts to video; insufflator makes space for laparoscopy."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gastro endoscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT741 - THERAPEUTIC EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gastro endoscope**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gastro Endoscope (Definition):** A **gastrointestinal (GI) endoscope** is a specialized, minimally invasive, fiber-optic or video-based therapeutic and diagnostic medical instrument designed to be inserted through a natural orifice (mouth or anus) to permit direct visualization, illumination, biopsy, and therapeutic intervention of the oesophagus, stomach, duodenum, jejunum, ileum, colon, and the biliary/pancreatic ducts.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the gastro endoscope is classified as a **Therapeutic Equipment** under the broader category of *Endoscopic / Minimally Invasive Surgical (MIS) Devices* that combine:
- **Optics** (image relay)
- **Photonics** (cold light transmission)
- **Micro-electronics** (CCD/CMOS imaging)
- **Biomechanics** (flexible insertion tube, angulation system)
- **Fluidics** (suction, air, water, CO₂ insufflation)

## 1.2 Classification of Gastro Endoscopes

| Sub-class | Insertion Route | Region Examined | Typical Length |
|---|---|---|---|
| Upper GI / Gastroscope / OGD scope | Oral → Oesophagus | Oesophagus, Stomach, Duodenum (D1, D2) | ~1000–1100 mm |
| Colonoscope | Anal | Rectum, Sigmoid, Descending, Transverse, Ascending colon, Terminal ileum | ~1300–1700 mm |
| Sigmoidoscope (flexible) | Anal | Rectum, Sigmoid colon | ~600–700 mm |
| Duodenoscope (Side-viewing) | Oral | Ampulla of Vater, Biliary & Pancreatic ducts (ERCP) | ~1240 mm |
| Enteroscope (Double/Single Balloon) | Oral or Anal | Jejunum, Ileum (Small bowel) | ~2000–2300 mm |
| Echoendoscope (EUS) | Oral or Anal | GI wall layers, Adjacent organs | ~1200–1500 mm |
| Capsule Endoscope | Swallowed | Entire small bowel | ~26 mm × 11 mm |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Plumbing Snake With a Camera"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of a gastro endoscope as a **flexible plumber's snake that has been upgraded with three superpowers**:
> 1. **A flashlight** at the tip (cold light source via fiber-optic bundle).
> 2. **A smartphone camera** that streams HD video to a monitor (CCD/CMOS image sensor).
> 3. **A miniature robotic arm** that can grab a tissue sample, stop bleeding, or remove a polyp (biopsy/working channel + instruments).
>
> The bending section at the tip acts like the **human wrist** — it can turn up, down, left, and right so the doctor can "look around corners" inside the 9-metre long intestinal tube without ever making a single surgical cut.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics Highlighted

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Parameters (Industry-typical, KTU Board-expected values):**
> - **Outer diameter of insertion tube:** **8.0 – 13.0 mm** (pediatric: 5.0 – 7.0 mm)
> - **Working length:** **1000 mm (upper GI)** | **1700 mm (colonoscope)**
> - **Field of View (FOV):** **120° – 140°**
> - **Depth of Field (DOF):** **3 – 100 mm**
> - **Bending range:** **Up 180°–210° / Down 90°–180° / Left 100°–160° / Right 100°–160°**
> - **Working channel diameter:** **2.0 – 4.2 mm** (most common: 2.8 mm and 3.7 mm)
> - **Light source colour temperature:** **5500 – 6500 K** (Xenon arc)
> - **Illumination output:** **≥ 300 W Xenon equivalent**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomical Block-Level Architecture of a Modern Video Gastroscope

A modern video gastro endoscope has **six major functional subsystems** that the KTU examiner expects you to enumerate, label, and explain:

### A. Insertion Tube (Flexible Shaft)
- Constructed of a **multi-layer helical stainless-steel mesh** (braided sheath) for torque stability and crush resistance.
- Inner lining: **PTFE / polymer liner** (low friction).
- Outer coating: **Medical-grade elastomer** (biocompatible, hydrophilic in some scopes).
- Houses internally: **light guide bundle, image bundle or electrical wires for CCD, working channel, air channel, water channel, suction channel, angulation wires (4 wires)**.

### B. Bending Section (Distal Articulating Tip)
- A short (≈ 50–80 mm) **segmented, articulated** region between the insertion tube and the rigid distal tip.
- Controlled by **four angulation wires** running through the full length of the scope, operated by **two rotary knobs** (Up/Down and Left/Right) on the control body.
- Acts as a **remote-centre-motion (RCM) joint** — it allows 4-way bending so the tip can navigate the tortuous GI tract.

### C. Distal End (Rigid Tip)
Contains the:
- **Objective lens** (wide-angle, ~140° FOV).
- **CCD/CMOS image sensor** (in video scopes) — typical pixel count: 100 K to 1.2 M (HD).
- **Light guide fibre termination** (illumination window).
- **Air/Water nozzle** (lens washing).
- **Working channel outlet** (instrument exit).
- **Auxiliary water jet** (optional, for high-pressure lens cleaning in colonoscopy).

### D. Control Body (Hand-Piece)
The "steering wheel" of the endoscope. Houses:
- **Angulation control knobs** (Up/Down, Left/Right; some models have a third "lock" lever).
- **Suction button** (white/blue).
- **Air/Water button** (red/blue, with two-stage press: air → water).
- **Working channel inlet (biopsy port)** with rubber flap.
- **Electrical connector** to the video processor.
- **Light guide connector** to the light source.

### E. Universal Cord / Light Guide Cable
- Contains fibre bundle, electrical wires, and air/water/suction tubing.
- Connects to the **Light Source + Video Processor unit (often combined)**.

### F. Light Source + Video Processor + Monitor
- **Light source:** 300 W Xenon arc lamp OR high-power LED.
- **Video processor:** digitises, enhances (structure enhancement, color tone, noise reduction), and outputs to the monitor in HD/4K.

## 2.2 Light Transmission in Gastro Endoscopes

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "cold light"?** The light source is outside the body. Light is carried to the tip via a non-coherent **fibre-optic bundle**, so the heat of the lamp never enters the patient — this is the principle of **"Cold Light Illumination."**

### Types of Fibre Bundles

| Bundle Type | Purpose | Structure |
|---|---|---|
| **Coherent (Image) Bundle** | Carries the actual image (in older fibrescopes) | Fibres aligned in identical order at both ends |
| **Non-coherent (Light) Bundle** | Carries illumination only | Fibres randomly arranged; cross-section irrelevant |

### Numerical Aperture (NA) of Optical Fibres
The light-gathering ability of a single fibre is given by:

$$\text{NA} = n_0 \, \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}$$

Where:
- $n_0$ = refractive index of the surrounding medium (≈ 1.0 in air)
- $n_1$ = refractive index of the **core**
- $n_2$ = refractive index of the **cladding**
- $\theta_a$ = **acceptance angle** (half-angle of the cone of light that can enter the fibre)

> **KTU examiner tip:** For TIR to occur inside the fibre, $n_1 > n_2$. Typical medical fibre values: $n_1 = 1.62$, $n_2 = 1.52$, giving $\text{NA} \approx 0.56$.

## 2.3 Image Formation Pathway

### 2.3.1 Fibrescope (Older Technology)
$$\text{Object} \;\to\; \text{Objective Lens} \;\to\; \text{Coherent Image Bundle} \;\to\; \text{Eyepiece / Camera Coupler} \;\to\; \text{Eye / CCD}$$

### 2.3.2 Video Endoscope (Modern Technology)
$$\text{Object} \;\to\; \text{Objective Lens} \;\to\; \text{CCD/CMOS Sensor (at tip)} \;\to\; \text{Electrical signal} \;\to\; \text{Video Processor} \;\to\; \text{HD Monitor}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** In modern video endoscopes the **image is captured AT the distal tip by a CCD/CMOS chip**, eliminating the fragile coherent fibre bundle. This dramatically improves resolution (SD → HD → 4K) and durability.

## 2.4 Suction, Air, Water, and Insufflation

The endoscope provides four fluidic channels:

| Channel | Function | Typical Flow / Pressure |
|---|---|---|
| **Suction channel** | Removes fluid, blood, debris; negative pressure | -300 to -500 mmHg |
| **Air channel** | Insufflates air to distend the lumen | ~0.5 – 1.5 L/min |
| **Water channel** | Washes the objective lens | Pressurised bottle (~200–500 mmHg) |
| **Auxiliary water jet** | High-pressure colon mucosa wash (colonoscopy) | 30 – 200 mL/min |
| **CO₂ insufflation** (modern) | Faster absorption, less post-op pain | 0.5 – 3.5 L/min |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Parameter Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Value | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Numerical Aperture | $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^{2} - n_2^{2}}$ | dimensionless | Fibre selection |
| 2 | Critical angle | $\sin\theta_c = n_2 / n_1$ | degrees | TIR condition |
| 3 | Acceptance angle | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(\text{NA}/n_0)$ | degrees | Maximum entry angle |
| 4 | FOV (endoscope) | typical **120° – 140°** | degrees | Tip lens design |
| 5 | DOF (endoscope) | **3 – 100 mm** | mm | Focus range |
| 6 | Insertion tube length | 1000 (upper) / 1700 (colon) | mm | Application |
| 7 | Working channel | 2.8 / 3.7 / 4.2 | mm | Instrument compatibility |
| 8 | Angulation | U 180–210 / D 90–180 | degrees | Navigation |
| 9 | Bending radius (curved) | ~ 15 – 25 mm | mm | Tight colon loops |
| 10 | Xenon colour temp | 5500 – 6500 K | K | True-colour rendering |
| 11 | Frame rate (video) | 25 / 30 / 60 fps | fps | Smooth motion |
| 12 | Sensor resolution | 100 K → 4 M pixels | pixels | Image quality |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical TIR condition:** $\;n_1 > n_2\;$ is the **single most important prerequisite** for light to propagate through the optical fibre. Without this, the cold light fibre bundle will leak light, and the image bundle will lose brightness — a **classic KTU 2-mark conceptual question**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Numerical Aperture & Acceptance Angle of a GI Endoscope Light Fibre

**Step 1 — Define the geometry.**  
Consider a step-index optical fibre with core index $n_1$ and cladding index $n_2$, surrounded by a medium of index $n_0$ (air ≈ 1.0). A light ray strikes the flat input face at an external half-angle $\theta_a$ to the fibre axis.

**Step 2 — Apply Snell's Law at the input face.**  
The ray refracts into the core at an angle $\theta_1$ to the axis:

$$
n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_1
$$

**Step 3 — Relate $\theta_1$ to the angle of incidence at the core–cladding interface.**  
Inside the core, the ray strikes the core–cladding boundary at an incidence angle $\theta_i = 90° - \theta_1$. For **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** to occur at this interface, we need:

$$
\theta_i \geq \theta_c
$$

where $\theta_c$ is the critical angle given by $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$.

**Step 4 — Write the TIR inequality.**

$$
90° - \theta_1 \;\geq\; \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)
$$

$$
\sin\theta_1 \;\leq\; \cos\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)\right] \;=\; \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back using Snell's Law.**  

$$
\sin\theta_a = \frac{n_1}{n_0}\sin\theta_1 \;\leq\; \frac{n_1}{n_0}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Define the Numerical Aperture.**  
The maximum value of $\sin\theta_a$ is defined as the Numerical Aperture:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{NA} = n_0 \sin\theta_a^{\max} = \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}\;}
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical example for a typical GI cold-light fibre.**  
Given $n_1 = 1.62$ (silica core), $n_2 = 1.52$ (silicone cladding), and $n_0 = 1.0$ (air):

$$
\text{NA} = \sqrt{(1.62)^{2} - (1.52)^{2}} = \sqrt{2.6244 - 2.3104} = \sqrt{0.3140} \approx 0.5603
$$

$$
\theta_a^{\max} = \sin^{-1}(0.5603) \approx 34.07°
$$

> **Interpretation:** Any light ray entering the fibre at an angle **less than 34.07°** to the axis will undergo TIR and successfully propagate to the endoscope tip. Rays steeper than this are refracted into the cladding and lost — a direct reason why light-source coupling efficiency matters.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Fibrescope vs. Video Endoscope

| Parameter | Fibrescope | Video Endoscope |
|---|---|---|
| Image carrier | Coherent fibre bundle | Electrical cable (CCD at tip) |
| Resolution | ~ 20 K – 40 K pixels (bundle) | 100 K → 4 M pixels (HD/4K) |
| Fragility | High (fibre breakage → black dots) | Low |
| Recording | Camera coupler needed | Built-in |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Weight / ergonomics | Heavier | Lighter, ergonomic |
| Image quality | Adequate for diagnosis | Excellent, supports NBI / chromo |

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Upper GI vs. Colonoscope

| Parameter | Upper GI / Gastroscope | Colonoscope |
|---|---|---|
| Working length | 1000 – 1100 mm | 1300 – 1700 mm |
| Outer diameter | 8.0 – 10.0 mm | 11.0 – 13.0 mm |
| FOV | 120° – 140° | 140° – 170° (wide) |
| Angulation U/D | 210° / 90° | 180° / 180° |
| Primary use | Oesophagus, stomach, duodenum | Colon, terminal ileum |
| Special features | Side-viewing variant for ERCP | Auxiliary water jet, NBI |
| Typical procedure time | 10 – 20 min | 20 – 45 min |

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Xenon vs. LED Light Source

| Parameter | Xenon Arc Lamp | High-Power LED |
|---|---|---|
| Colour temperature | ~ 6000 K (true daylight) | 5000 – 6500 K |
| Lifetime | ~ 500 – 1000 hrs | ~ 10 000 – 30 000 hrs |
| Power consumption | High (300 W) | Low (50 – 100 W) |
| Heat output | High → liquid light guide needed | Low → can use regular cable |
| Cost | Higher running cost | Lower running cost |
| Spectral quality | Excellent, broadband | Excellent, tunable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Complete Video Gastro Endoscopy System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient GI Tract] --> B[Distal Tip of Endoscope]
    B --> C[Objective Lens]
    C --> D[CCD/CMOS Image Sensor]
    D --> E[Video Processor]
    E --> F[HD Medical Monitor]
    E --> G[Image Recording / PACS]
    B --> H[Light Guide Fibre Bundle]
    H --> I[Xenon / LED Light Source]
    F -.feedback.-> J[Endoscopist]
    B --> K[Working Channel]
    K --> L[Biopsy Forceps / Snare / Clip Applier]
    B --> M[Air / Water / Suction Channels]
    M --> N[Insufflator / Suction Pump / Water Bottle]
    J --> O[Angulation Control Knobs]
    O --> P[Four Pull Wires in Insertion Tube]
    P --> B
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **endoscopist (J)** watches the **HD monitor (F)** and uses the **angulation knobs (O)** to steer the **distal tip (B)** via **pull wires (P)**. The **light source (I)** illuminates the mucosa through the **light guide (H)**. The **video processor (E)** digitises the signal from the **CCD/CMOS (D)**. **Therapeutic instruments (L)** such as biopsy forceps are passed through the **working channel (K)**. **Air / water / suction (M, N)** keep the lumen inflated and the lens clean.

## 4.2 Cross-Section of the Insertion Tube (Detailed Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["INSERTION TUBE CROSS-SECTION (outer to inner)"]
        A1["Outer Sheath: Medical-grade Elastomer"]
        A2["Braid Layer 1: Stainless Steel Helical Mesh"]
        A3["Braid Layer 2: Stainless Steel Counter-Helical Mesh"]
        A4["Inner Liner: PTFE / Polymer"]
        A5["Air Channel (annular space)"]
        A6["Water Channel (annular space)"]
        A7["Suction / Working Channel (largest, 2.8-4.2 mm)"]
        A8["Light Guide Fibre Bundle (illumination)"]
        A9["Electrical Wires for CCD + Angulation Wires (4x)"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Distal Tip Layout (End-On View — Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T["DISTAL TIP (end-on)"]
        T1["Light Guide Window 1"]
        T2["Light Guide Window 2"]
        T3["Objective Lens with CCD/CMOS"]
        T4["Air/Water Nozzle"]
        T5["Working Channel Outlet"]
        T6["Auxiliary Water Jet (optional)"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Functional State Machine — Endoscope Steering Action

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Neutral
    Neutral --> Up: U/D knob CW
    Neutral --> Down: U/D knob CCW
    Neutral --> Left: L/R knob CCW
    Neutral --> Right: L/R knob CW
    Up --> UpRight: combine knobs
    Up --> UpLeft: combine knobs
    Down --> DownRight: combine knobs
    Down --> DownLeft: combine knobs
    UpRight --> Neutral: knobs released
    UpLeft --> Neutral: knobs released
    DownRight --> Neutral: knobs released
    DownLeft --> Neutral: knobs released
    Right --> UpRight: combine knobs
    Left --> UpLeft: combine knobs
    Right --> DownRight: combine knobs
    Left --> DownLeft: combine knobs
```

## 4.5 ERCP Side-Viewing Duodenoscope (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Patient mouth] --> P1[Oesophagus]
    P1 --> P2[Stomach]
    P2 --> P3[Duodenum D1]
    P3 --> P4[Ampulla of Vater]
    P4 --> P5[Biliary Ducts / Pancreatic Duct]
    D[Side-viewing Duodenoscope tip] -.views.-> P4
    D --> P5
```

> **Key fact:** A standard gastroscope looks **forward (forward-viewing)**. A duodenoscope used for **ERCP** looks **sideways (side-viewing, 90° to the axis)** so that the working channel can be aligned with the **ampulla of Vater** for cannulation of the bile/pancreatic ducts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse a *fibrescope* with a *video endoscope* — image transmission method is the discriminator (coherent bundle vs. CCD at tip).
> 2. **Do NOT** claim the light source lamp is inside the patient. KTU specifically tests the **"Cold Light Principle"** — light is generated outside and piped in via a non-coherent fibre bundle.
> 3. **Do NOT** omit the **TIR condition** ($n_1 > n_2$) when explaining fibre-optic light transmission — this is a guaranteed 2-mark item.
> 4. **Do NOT** confuse the **acceptance angle** $\theta_a$ with the **critical angle** $\theta_c$. They are *different* angles measured *in different media*.
> 5. In a question on the **bending section**, always mention the **four pull wires** and the **two control knobs** — partial answers score 0.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is meant by "Cold Light" in gastro endoscopy? Why is it essential?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
"Cold light" refers to the principle of delivering illumination to the interior of the GI tract from an **external light source** (typically a **300 W Xenon arc lamp** or high-power LED) through a **non-coherent fibre-optic bundle** within the endoscope. The light is intense and bright at the mucosa, but because the heat-generating lamp is physically outside the patient's body, **no thermal energy enters the tissue**. This is essential because (i) it prevents **mucosal burns and thermal injury** to the delicate GI lining, and (ii) it allows very high luminous intensity for image quality without compromising patient safety. *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for fibre bundle transport, 1 mark for safety rationale.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between a coherent and a non-coherent fibre-optic bundle as used in a gastro endoscope.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Feature | Coherent Bundle | Non-Coherent Bundle |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Carries the **image** | Carries **illumination** only |
| Fibre arrangement | **Identical spatial order** at both ends (pixels preserved) | **Random** arrangement (no image to preserve) |
| Application | Image transmission in **fibrescopes** | Light delivery in **all modern scopes** |
| Cost | High (precise alignment) | Low |

*(Award 1 mark per correct row, 3 marks maximum.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 Scheme Module-Internal Choice: **Answer ANY ONE** of the two questions below.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete system architecture of a modern video gastro endoscope**, clearly identifying the following subsystems: insertion tube, bending section, distal tip, control body, light source, video processor, and monitor. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **expression for the Numerical Aperture (NA)** of a step-index optical fibre in terms of the core and cladding refractive indices, and compute the **acceptance angle** for a fibre with $n_1 = 1.62$ and $n_2 = 1.52$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):** A video gastro endoscope is a flexible fibre-optic/video instrument used to visualise the upper or lower GI tract. Unlike the older fibrescope, it places the image sensor (CCD/CMOS) at the **distal tip**, eliminating the coherent image bundle.

**Block Diagram & Description (6 marks):**

1. **Insertion tube (flexible shaft)** — a multi-layer sheath (outer elastomer + two counter-wound stainless-steel braids + inner PTFE liner) that houses the light guide, electrical wires, and fluidic channels. *(1 mark)*

2. **Bending section** — the articulated distal segment (~50–80 mm) bent by **four pull wires** controlled by the angulation knobs. Provides 4-way steering (Up/Down/Left/Right). *(1 mark)*

3. **Distal tip** — rigid end containing the **objective lens + CCD/CMOS sensor, light-guide windows, air/water nozzle, and the working channel outlet**. *(1 mark)*

4. **Control body (hand-piece)** — has **angulation knobs, suction button, air/water button, working channel inlet, and electrical connector.** *(1 mark)*

5. **Light source** — a **300 W Xenon arc lamp** (or modern LED) generating high-intensity, daylight-spectrum light (~6000 K), coupled into the light guide. *(1 mark)*

6. **Video processor + HD monitor** — receives the CCD signal, performs digital enhancement (structure enhancement, color tone), and displays the image in real time on a medical-grade HD monitor. Also routes signals to a recording/PACS system. *(1 mark)*

**Conclusion (optional 0.5 mark):** The endoscopist manipulates the control body, watches the monitor, and inserts therapeutic instruments through the working channel for biopsy or therapy.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $n_1 = 1.62$, $n_2 = 1.52$, $n_0 = 1.0$ (air).

**Step 1 — Critical angle (1 mark):**

$$
\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.52}{1.62} = 0.9383
$$

$$
\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.9383) \approx 69.83°
$$

**Step 2 — Numerical Aperture formula (3 marks):**

Starting from Snell's law at the input face and applying the TIR condition at the core–cladding interface:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}\;}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation (1 mark):**

$$
\text{NA} = \sqrt{(1.62)^2 - (1.52)^2} = \sqrt{2.6244 - 2.3104} = \sqrt{0.3140} \approx 0.560
$$

**Step 4 — Acceptance angle (2 marks):**

$$
\theta_a = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\text{NA}}{n_0}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.560) \approx 34.05°
$$

**Final answer (0.5 mark):** Any light ray entering the cold-light fibre at an angle **less than ~34° to the axis** will propagate by TIR and reach the endoscope tip. The acceptance half-angle of the fibre is **≈ 34°**, and its NA is **≈ 0.56**.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **"Bending Section and Angulation Mechanism"** of a gastro endoscope. How is 4-way steering achieved with only **two control knobs**? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable comparison, explain the **differences between an upper GI gastroscope, a colonoscope, and a side-viewing duodenoscope** used for ERCP. List at least **three distinguishing parameters** for each. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):** The bending section is the short, articulated segment at the distal end of a flexible endoscope, typically **50–80 mm** long. It allows the **rigid distal tip** (housing the camera and light guides) to be steered in multiple directions inside the tortuous GI tract.

**Construction (2 marks):** It is made of a series of **segmented metal rings** linked by **rivets or short links**, so that pulling on one side shortens that side and lengthens the opposite, creating a smooth arc. The four pull wires terminate at the tip of this section.

**4-Way steering using 2 knobs (3 marks):**  
- **Two control knobs** on the control body (one for Up/Down, one for Left/Right) are mechanically linked through a **chain/sprocket assembly** to the four pull wires.  
- Turning the **Up/Down knob** pulls the **upper pair** of wires and releases the **lower pair** → tip bends **up**, and vice versa.  
- Turning the **Left/Right knob** does the same with the **left and right pairs** → tip bends **left** or **right**.  
- By **turning both knobs simultaneously**, the operator can obtain **diagonal bending** (e.g., up-left, down-right), giving true 4-way steering. This is analogous to the **remote-centre-motion (RCM)** mechanism in robotic surgery.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** This elegant wire-pulley arrangement is what enables the endoscope to navigate the entire 9 m of human intestine from a single ergonomic hand-piece.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 Marks

| Parameter | Upper GI Gastroscope | Colonoscope | Side-Viewing Duodenoscope |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Working length** | ~ 1000 – 1100 mm | ~ 1300 – 1700 mm | ~ 1240 mm |
| **Outer diameter** | 8.0 – 10.0 mm | 11.0 – 13.0 mm | 10.0 – 12.0 mm |
| **Viewing direction** | Forward (0°) | Forward (0°) | **Sideways (90°)** |
| **Typical FOV** | 120° – 140° | 140° – 170° | 100° – 120° |
| **Working channel** | 2.8 mm (therapeutic 3.7) | 3.7 / 4.2 mm | 3.7 / 4.2 mm |
| **Special features** | NBI, magnification | Auxiliary water jet, HD | **Elevator** for cannulation |
| **Primary use** | Oesophagus, stomach, duodenum | Colon, terminal ileum | **ERCP** (cannulate ampulla of Vater) |
| **Angulation U/D** | 210° / 90° | 180° / 180° | 120° / 90° |

*(Award 1 mark for a clear introduction, 4 marks for the parameter table, 1 mark for explaining why the duodenoscope is side-viewing (to align with the ampulla of Vater in the duodenal wall), and 1 mark for a concluding statement.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Gastro Endoscope**

- [ ] A gastro endoscope is a **flexible fibre-optic/video device** for diagnosing and treating GI tract diseases without surgery.
- [ ] The **light source is external** — light is piped in via a **non-coherent fibre bundle** (the **"Cold Light" Principle**).
- [ ] The **image is captured at the distal tip** in modern scopes (CCD/CMOS); older fibrescopes used a **coherent fibre bundle**.
- [ ] The **insertion tube** has multi-layer construction: elastomer + two SS braids + PTFE liner.
- [ ] The **bending section** uses **four pull wires and two control knobs** to give 4-way steering.
- [ ] The **working channel** (2.8 / 3.7 / 4.2 mm) carries biopsy forceps, snares, clips, etc.
- [ ] **Air / water / suction** channels keep the lumen inflated and the lens clean.
- [ ] **Numerical Aperture:** $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^{2} - n_2^{2}}$ — derived from Snell's law + TIR condition.
- [ ] **TIR condition:** $n_1 > n_2$ — *must be stated explicitly* in any derivation.
- [ ] **Side-viewing duodenoscope** is required for **ERCP** (ampulla of Vater cannulation).
- [ ] **Colonoscope** is longer (≈ 1700 mm) and has an **auxiliary water jet** for mucosal cleaning.
- [ ] **Xenon** (~6000 K, broadband) is the gold-standard light source; **LED** is replacing it for long life.
- [ ] **NBI (Narrow Band Imaging)** and **chromoendoscopy** are advanced image-enhancement modes used for early cancer detection.
- [ ] Always mention **sterilisation / high-level disinfection** (e.g., glutaraldehyde, peracetic acid, or auto-disinfector) — it is a KTU favourite 2-mark question on patient safety.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bronchoscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT741 - THERAPEUTIC EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bronchoscope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bronchoscope** is a specialized **endoscopic instrument** designed for the direct visualization, diagnosis, and therapeutic intervention of the **tracheobronchial tree** (larynx, vocal cords, trachea, carina, main bronchi, lobar bronchi, and segmental bronchi down to sub-segmental levels) of the human respiratory system.

The term is derived from the Greek words *bronchos* (windpipe) and *skopein* (to look). In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the bronchoscope is classified under **diagnostic and therapeutic rigid/flexible endoscopic equipment** that integrates principles of **fiber-optic light transmission, miniaturized imaging, and minimally invasive surgical access**.

### 1.2 Clinical & Engineering Classification

| Parameter | Rigid Bronchoscope | Flexible (Fiberoptic) Bronchoscope |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Outer Diameter** | 9–14 mm | 2.8–6.3 mm |
| **Working Length** | ~430 mm | ~550–600 mm |
| **Patient Sedation** | General anesthesia | Local anesthesia + conscious sedation |
| **Primary Use** | Therapeutic (foreign body, tumor debulking) | Diagnostic (biopsy, lavage, EBUS) |
| **Image Quality** | Direct optical | Fiber-optic / Video CCD |
| **Distal Tip Deflection** | None (rigid) | Up-Bend **180°**, Down-Bend **130°** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of the bronchoscope as a **"plumber's flexible inspection camera"** that doctors send down a patient's airway. Imagine threading a long, bendable, self-illuminated cable (similar to a USB endoscope camera used to inspect car engines or drain pipes) through the windpipe to take **high-resolution pictures**, **collect tissue samples (biopsies)**, or **remove blockages** — all without making a single surgical cut on the chest.

The "illumination" comes from an external **cold light source** (xenon/LED) whose light is carried into the airway via a bundle of thin glass fibers, while the returning image is carried back through a separate coherent fiber bundle to the examiner's eyepiece or a video camera head.

### 1.4 Engineering Significance

* **Optical Engineering:** Coherent fiber-optic bundle alignment (pixel-to-pixel mapping) with **~10,000–40,000 individual fibers** of diameter **5–10 µm** each.
* **Mechanical Engineering:** Distal tip articulation using **two-way or four-way angulation cables** pulled by thumb levers at the control body.
* **Biomedical Engineering:** Biocompatible materials (surgical stainless steel, polyurethane sheath) sterilizable by **autoclaving** or **Cidex (glutaraldehyde 2%)** immersion.
* **Electronic Engineering:** Modern **video bronchoscopes** replace the fiber bundle with a **CCD/CMOS image sensor (pixel size 2–5 µm)** at the distal tip, providing HD/4K imaging.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Structured Logic

The bronchoscope operates on **three coupled physical principles**:

1. **Illumination Principle — Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**
   * Light from a **xenon arc lamp (300 W, color temperature 6000 K)** or **LED source** is focused into a **non-coherent (illumination) fiber bundle**.
   * Each fiber has a **core (n₁ ≈ 1.62, fused silica)** and **cladding (n₂ ≈ 1.48)**. Light is guided by TIR when the angle of incidence exceeds the **critical angle (θc)**.

2. **Image Transmission Principle — Coherent Fiber Bundle**
   * A second, **coherent (image) bundle** carries the reflected light from the mucosa back to the eyepiece.
   * "Coherent" means each fiber at the **distal end** occupies the **same relative position** at the **proximal end**, preserving the 2D image.

3. **Articulation Principle — Cable-Pulley Mechanism**
   * Stainless-steel control wires run inside the flexible shaft from the **angulation lever (thumb control)** to anchor points just behind the bending section.
   * Pulling the lever shortens one cable and slackens the opposite one, producing **single-plane or two-plane bending**.

### 2.2 Anatomical Reach & Resolution

| Anatomical Target | Generation from Trachea | Bronchoscope Type Required |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Larynx, Vocal cords | 0 | Rigid or Flexible |
| Trachea, Main carina | 1 | Flexible |
| Lobar bronchi (RUL, LUL, etc.) | 2 | Flexible |
| Segmental bronchi | 3–4 | Standard flexible |
| Sub-segmental (peripheral lesions) | 5–6 | **Ultra-thin** flexible (OD < 3.5 mm) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantitative relationships governing fiber-optic image transmission in a bronchoscope are summarized below. Memorize the **first two equations** — they are the most frequently asked in KTU ESE.

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Quantity} & \textbf{Formula} & \textbf{Physical Meaning} \\
\hline
\text{Numerical Aperture (NA)} & NA = n_0 \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} & \text{Light-gathering ability} \\
\hline
\text{Critical Angle} & \theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right) & \text{Min. angle for TIR} \\
\hline
\text{Resolution (Rayleigh)} & d = \dfrac{0.61\,\lambda}{NA} & \text{Smallest resolvable detail} \\
\hline
\text{Image Fiber Diameter} & d_f = 5{-}10\ \mu m & \text{Determines pixel count} \\
\hline
\text{Frame Rate} & f = 25{-}60\ \text{fps} & \text{Video smoothness} \\
\hline
\text{Total Fiber Count} & N = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4 d_f^2} & \text{Fibers in bundle} \\
\hline
\text{Bending Radius} & R \ge 30\ \text{mm} & \text{Prevents fiber fracture} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TYPING NOTE:** All formulas above have been rendered with $\vert$ and $\dfrac$ so they never break the markdown table border. When writing in your answer script, always use `sin⁻¹` or `arcsin` written out — never the bare vertical-bar notation, as examiners often misread it.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Pulmonology & Thoracic Surgery:** Diagnosis of lung cancer, tuberculosis, sarcoidosis, foreign-body extraction.
* **Critical Care (ICU):** Bedside bronchoscopy for ventilated patients to remove mucus plugs, perform **bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL)**.
* **Anesthesia:** Confirmation of **double-lumen tube** placement and one-lung ventilation.
* **Recent Advances:** **Endobronchial Ultrasound (EBUS)** — adds a 7.5–12 MHz radial or convex ultrasound transducer at the tip to image **peribronchial lymph nodes** for transbronchial needle aspiration (TBNA) and lung cancer staging.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Acceptance Cone (Numerical Aperture) of an Illumination Fiber

**Problem Setup:** A glass optical fiber used in the bronchoscope's light bundle has **core refractive index n₁ = 1.62** and **cladding refractive index n₂ = 1.48**. The fiber tip is immersed in air (n₀ = 1.00). Calculate the **numerical aperture (NA)** and the **maximum acceptance angle (θₐ)** of light that can enter the fiber.

**Step 1 — State the definition of NA at the air-fiber interface.**

$$
NA = n_0 \sin \theta_a
$$

where θₐ is the half-angle of the cone of light that can be coupled into the fiber from the outside medium (air, n₀ = 1).

**Step 2 — State the internal relationship using Snell's law between core and cladding.**

$$
NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}
$$

This comes from applying Snell's law at the air-core and core-cladding boundaries and substituting the critical angle condition $\sin \theta_c = n_2/n_1$.

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
NA = \sqrt{(1.62)^2 - (1.48)^2}
$$

$$
NA = \sqrt{2.6244 - 2.1904} = \sqrt{0.4340}
$$

$$
NA \approx 0.6587
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the maximum acceptance angle θₐ.**

$$
\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA) = \sin^{-1}(0.6587) \approx 41.16^{\circ}
$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation.**

> [!NOTE]
> A cone of half-angle **41.16°** enters the fiber. Any light striking the fiber face at a steeper angle will refract into the core at less than the critical angle θc and **leak into the cladding**, failing to illuminate the airway. This is why bronchoscope cold-light sources use **focused parabolic reflectors** that produce a converging beam matched to this acceptance cone for maximum transmission efficiency.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Angle for TIR

**Problem Setup:** Same fiber as above. Compute the critical angle θc inside the core at the core–cladding interface.

**Step 1 — Critical angle condition.**

At the core-cladding interface, TIR begins when the refracted ray runs along the boundary, i.e., the refracted angle equals **90°**.

$$
n_1 \sin \theta_c = n_2 \sin 90^{\circ}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for θc.**

$$
\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.48}{1.62} = 0.9136
$$

$$
\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.9136) \approx 66.04^{\circ}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-check with NA.**

$$
NA = n_1 \cos \theta_c = 1.62 \times \cos(66.04^{\circ}) = 1.62 \times 0.4067 \approx 0.659 \ \checkmark
$$

The two methods agree to three decimal places, confirming our numerical aperture.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spatial Resolution (Rayleigh Criterion)

**Problem Setup:** Determine the **smallest resolvable mucosal feature** when the bronchoscope is used with **white light of mean wavelength λ = 550 nm**.

**Step 1 — Apply the Rayleigh resolution formula.**

$$
d = \frac{0.61\,\lambda}{NA}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numbers.**

$$
d = \frac{0.61 \times 550 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}}{0.6587}
$$

$$
d = \frac{3.355 \times 10^{-7}}{0.6587} \approx 5.09 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m} = 0.509\ \mu m
$$

**Step 3 — Practical Implication.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A flexible bronchoscope can theoretically resolve features as small as **~0.5 µm** on the bronchial mucosa. In practice, however, the **fiber diameter (5–10 µm)** of the coherent bundle becomes the **limiting factor** because each fiber acts as one "pixel." Hence the **effective resolution is limited to the fiber pitch (~7 µm)**, not the diffraction limit. Modern **video bronchoscopes** using distal CCD/CMOS sensors overcome this, achieving **true HD resolution of ~1920 × 1080 pixels** with pixel size 2–3 µm.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Total Fiber Count in the Coherent Bundle

**Problem Setup:** The coherent image bundle has an **outer diameter D = 1.5 mm** and individual **fiber diameter d_f = 8 µm**. Estimate the number of resolvable image pixels.

**Step 1 — Geometric formula.**

$$
N = \frac{\pi D^2 / 4}{d_f^2} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4 d_f^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert units consistently.**

$$
D = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m},\ \ d_f = 8 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
N = \frac{\pi \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4 \times (8 \times 10^{-6})^2}
$$

$$
N = \frac{\pi \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 64 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{7.069 \times 10^{-6}}{2.56 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

$$
N \approx 27{,}610\ \text{fibers}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

> A typical fiberoptic bronchoscope contains **~25,000–30,000 individual glass fibers** in the image bundle. This produces a **grainy, pixelated "honeycomb" image** when viewed closely — a hallmark identifying a fiberoptic (not video) bronchoscope. Higher-end scopes (e.g., Olympus BF-1T260) use **~40,000 fibers** for a smoother image.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Component-to-Function Mapping

| System Sub-Assembly | Component | Function | Typical Specification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Illumination Path | Xenon cold-light source | Provides high-intensity white light | 300 W, 6000 K |
| Illumination Path | Non-coherent fiber bundle | Carries light to distal tip | 1.5 mm OD, 20,000 fibers |
| Imaging Path | Coherent fiber bundle | Returns image to eyepiece | 1.5 mm OD, 25,000–40,000 fibers |
| Imaging Path | Objective lens (distal) | Focuses mucosa onto fiber face | f = 1.5 mm, FOV = 80°–120° |
| Imaging Path | Eyepiece / Camera adapter | Magnifies image | 10×–20× |
| Mechanical Path | Angulation cables | Bends distal tip | Up 180°, Down 130° |
| Mechanical Path | Insertion tube | Conduit for all components | OD 2.8–6.3 mm, length 600 mm |
| Working Channel | Biopsy/suction channel | Instruments & suction | ID 1.2–3.2 mm |
| Control Body | Suction valve, Lever, Buttons | User interface | Suction, angulation, freeze, image capture |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Flexible Video Bronchoscope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Cold Light Source XENON 300W] --> B[Light Guide Cable Connector]
    B --> C[Non Coherent Illumination Fiber Bundle]
    C --> D[Distal Tip Light Distribution Lens]
    D --> E[Bronchial Mucosa Tissue]
    E --> F[Distal Objective Lens FOV 100 deg]
    F --> G[Image Sensor CCD CMOS at Tip]
    G --> H[Electrical Signal Cable through Shaft]
    H --> I[Video Processor Image Enhancement]
    I --> J[HD LCD Monitor Display]
    K[Angulation Control Lever] --> L[Pull Wires Stainless Steel]
    L --> M[Bending Section Rubber Sleeve]
    M --> N[Distal Tip Deflection Up 180 Down 130]
    O[Working Channel Port Suction Biopsy] --> P[Instrument Channel ID 2.0 mm]
    P --> Q[Distal Channel Exit Nozzle]
    E --> Q
    Q --> O
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Light travels from the **external source (A)** down the **illumination bundle (C)** to illuminate the **mucosa (E)**. The reflected image is captured by the **distal objective (F)**, focused onto a **distal-tip CCD/CMOS sensor (G)** in a video bronchoscope, and the electronic signal is sent up the shaft (H) to the **video processor (I)** and finally to the **monitor (J)**. The **angulation lever (K)** controls the **bending section (M)** via pull wires. The **working channel (P)** allows the doctor to pass biopsy forceps, cytology brushes, or suction catheters out through the distal nozzle (Q) to interact with the mucosa (E).

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Rigid Bronchoscope Optical Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Light Source] --> B[Fiber Optic Light Carrier]
    B --> C[Proximal Light Prism Redirector]
    C --> D[Hollow Stainless Steel Tube Inner Dia 8 to 14 mm]
    D --> E[Distal Window Illumination Field]
    E --> F[Bronchial Tree Target]
    F --> G[Reflected Light Travels Back through Tube]
    G --> H[Proximal Eyepiece]
    H --> I[Direct View by Examiner or Camera Attachment]
    J[Side Port for Ventilation and Anesthesia] --- D
    K[Working Channel for Forceps and Suction] --- D
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rigid vs. Flexible — Key difference:** The rigid bronchoscope uses **direct line-of-sight optics** (Hopkins rod-lens telescope inserted into a hollow metal tube). It does **not** use a coherent fiber bundle for imaging — the doctor literally looks down the tube, often with a video camera attached at the proximal end. This gives a **brighter, higher-resolution image** but at the cost of patient comfort and reach.

---

### 4.3 Endobronchial Ultrasound (EBUS) — Convex Probe Variant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Distal Tip of EBUS Bronchoscope] --> B[Optical Window 30 deg Forward Oblique]
    A --> C[Balloons for Water Coupling Saline Filled]
    A --> D[Convex Ultrasound Transducer 7.5 MHz]
    D --> E[Ultrasound Beam Penetrates Airway Wall]
    E --> F[Peribronchial Lymph Node or Mass]
    F --> G[Echo Signal Returned to Transducer]
    G --> H[Ultrasound Processor B Mode Image]
    H --> I[Real Time Image Overlay with White Light]
    J[22 Gauge TBNA Needle Channel] --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **EBUS clinical note:** The convex-probe EBUS (e.g., Olympus BF-UC180F) enables **real-time transbronchial needle aspiration (TBNA)** of mediastinal lymph nodes — a critical procedure for **lung-cancer staging (N-staging)**. The balloon at the tip is inflated with saline to provide an **acoustic coupling medium** that displaces the air gap between the transducer and airway wall (air strongly reflects ultrasound).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023] Define a bronchoscope. List any two differences between a rigid and a flexible bronchoscope.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> A **bronchoscope** is a tubular endoscopic instrument used for direct visualization, diagnosis, and therapeutic intervention of the **tracheobronchial tree** (larynx, trachea, and bronchi).

| Aspect | Rigid | Flexible |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Patient preparation | General anesthesia | Local anesthesia + sedation |
| Optical principle | Hopkins rod-lens telescope (direct view) | Coherent fiber-optic bundle |
| Distal-tip angulation | None | Up 180°, Down 130° |

*(Any two differences = 2 marks; definition = 1 mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] What is meant by "coherent fiber bundle" in a flexible bronchoscope? Why is it essential for image transmission?** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **coherent fiber bundle** is an assembly of thousands of optical fibers in which **each fiber occupies the same relative spatial position at both the distal and proximal ends** of the bundle. This one-to-one mapping of fiber positions preserves the **two-dimensional image** of the mucosa at the distal end and reconstructs it faithfully at the eyepiece, enabling the examiner to visualize the airway in real time. *(Definition = 2 marks; image-fidelity explanation = 1 mark.)*

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Internal-Choice Note:** KTU 2024 ESE mandates that students answer **either** Q(A) **or** Q(B). Both are presented below with full solutions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Fiber-Optic Principle, NA Calculation, Block Diagram* [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **flexible fiber-optic bronchoscope**. List its **main components** and the function of each. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** An illumination fiber in the bronchoscope has a core refractive index **n₁ = 1.62** and cladding refractive index **n₂ = 1.48**. Calculate the **(i) numerical aperture, (ii) acceptance angle, and (iii) critical angle** of the fiber. The light source emits a mean wavelength of **550 nm**. Calculate the **(iv) theoretical spatial resolution** at the tissue surface. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q(A)(a)

**Construction (3 marks):**

The flexible fiber-optic bronchoscope consists of:

1. **Control body (handle):** Contains the angulation lever, suction valve, biopsy-port inlet, and eyepiece/camera adapter.
2. **Insertion tube (flexible shaft):** A watertight, biocompatible sheath (length ~600 mm, outer diameter 2.8–6.3 mm) housing two fiber bundles, two angulation cables, and the working channel.
3. **Bending section (distal articulated tip):** A short flexible rubber-sleeved segment (length 5–6 cm) that bends up by 180° and down by 130° via the control cables.
4. **Distal tip:** Houses the objective lens, light-distribution lens, and the working-channel exit nozzle.
5. **Light-guide cable:** Connects the scope to an external **xenon cold-light source (300 W)** via a **light-guide connector**.
6. **Working channel:** A hollow lumen (ID 1.2–3.2 mm) running the full length for suction and insertion of biopsy forceps, brushes, or laser fibers.

**Working (3 marks):**

* Light from the xenon source is focused into the **non-coherent (illumination) fiber bundle** and transmitted to the distal tip, where it emerges to illuminate the bronchial mucosa.
* Light reflected from the mucosa is collected by the **objective lens** at the distal tip and focused onto the **distal face of the coherent image bundle**.
* The coherent bundle transmits the image, fiber by fiber, to the proximal face, where it is magnified by the **eyepiece** (or captured by a video camera) for the examiner to view.
* The doctor manipulates the **angulation lever** to steer the tip through the branching airways and inserts instruments (forceps, needles) through the **working channel** to obtain tissue samples or perform therapy (laser, electrocautery, stent placement).

**Functions of main components (1 mark):** as tabulated in Section 3.5 above.

---

#### Model Solution for Q(A)(b)

**Given:** n₁ = 1.62, n₂ = 1.48, λ = 550 nm.

**(i) Numerical Aperture (2 marks):**

$$
NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.62)^2 - (1.48)^2}
$$

$$
NA = \sqrt{2.6244 - 2.1904} = \sqrt{0.4340} = 0.6587
$$

**(ii) Acceptance Angle (2 marks):**

$$
\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA) = \sin^{-1}(0.6587) \approx 41.16^{\circ}
$$

**(iii) Critical Angle (1.5 marks):**

$$
\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.48}{1.62}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.9136) \approx 66.04^{\circ}
$$

**(iv) Spatial Resolution (1.5 marks):**

$$
d = \frac{0.61\,\lambda}{NA} = \frac{0.61 \times 550 \times 10^{-9}}{0.6587}
$$

$$
d = \frac{3.355 \times 10^{-7}}{0.6587} \approx 5.09 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m} = 0.51\ \mu m
$$

> The theoretical diffraction-limited resolution is **~0.51 µm**, although the practical resolution of a fiber bronchoscope is limited to the **fiber-pitch (~7 µm)** of the coherent bundle.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Rigid Bronchoscope, Total Fiber Count, EBUS Concept* [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the **construction, working, and clinical applications** of a **rigid bronchoscope**. Compare it briefly with a flexible bronchoscope. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A flexible bronchoscope has a coherent image bundle of outer diameter **1.5 mm** composed of individual fibers each of diameter **8 µm**. Calculate the **approximate number of fibers** in the bundle, and hence estimate the **effective image resolution** of the scope. Briefly explain the principle and clinical use of **Endobronchial Ultrasound (EBUS)**. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q(B)(a)

**Construction (3 marks):**

A rigid bronchoscope is a **hollow stainless-steel or chrome-plated brass tube** with the following parts:

* **Proximal end:** Light carrier connector, **Hopkins rod-lens telescope** insertion port, eyepiece (or video camera mount), ventilation/side port for anesthetic gases.
* **Main body:** A rigid tube of inner diameter **8–14 mm** and length **~430 mm**, with a smooth bevelled distal tip.
* **Distal end:** Slots/openings to allow ventilation and passage of instruments.
* **Light carrier:** A fiber-optic light bundle screwed into the proximal side port that conveys light from the cold-light source to the distal end.

**Working (2 marks):**

* Illumination is provided by a **xenon cold-light source** transmitted through the **fiber-optic light carrier** to the distal end.
* The **Hopkins rod-lens telescope** (a series of rod-lens spacers between glass lenses) inserted into the rigid tube provides **bright, magnified, wide-angle direct view** of the trachea and main bronchi.
* Instruments (suction catheters, biopsy forceps, laser fibers, cryoprobes) are passed through a separate channel or alongside the telescope.
* Because the tube is **rigid**, the patient must be under **general anesthesia** with the neck hyperextended.

**Clinical Applications (1 mark):**

* Removal of **foreign bodies** (especially in children).
* Control of **massive hemoptysis** (bleeding).
* **Tumor debulking** with laser or electrocautery.
* **Stent placement** in malignant airway obstruction.
* Dilatation of **tracheal stenosis**.

**Comparison with Flexible Bronchoscope (1 mark):**

| Feature | Rigid | Flexible |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Anesthesia | General | Local |
| Reach | Main bronchi only | Up to sub-segmental |
| Image | Direct, bright | Fiber-optic, softer |
| Therapeutic power | High (large instruments) | Limited by channel size |

---

#### Model Solution for Q(B)(b)

**Number of Fibers (3 marks):**

$$
N = \frac{\pi D^2}{4 d_f^2} = \frac{\pi \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4 \times (8 \times 10^{-6})^2}
$$

$$
N = \frac{\pi \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6}}{2.56 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 27{,}610\ \text{fibers}
$$

**Effective Resolution (1 mark):**

Each fiber acts as one image pixel. The fiber pitch is ~8 µm, so the **effective image resolution is ~7–8 µm** at the mucosa, which is much coarser than the diffraction limit of 0.5 µm. This is why the **fiber-bundle image is "honeycomb-like"** and why modern **video bronchoscopes (with distal CCD/CMOS)** produce visibly sharper images.

**EBUS Principle and Use (3 marks):**

**Principle:** Endobronchial Ultrasound (EBUS) integrates a **miniature ultrasound transducer (7.5–12 MHz)** at the distal tip of a flexible bronchoscope. A **saline-filled balloon** is inflated around the probe to displace air (which strongly reflects ultrasound) and provide acoustic coupling. The transducer emits pulses that penetrate the airway wall and reflect back from **peribronchial structures** (lymph nodes, vessels, tumors), which are reconstructed into a real-time **B-mode ultrasound image**.

**Clinical Use:** Real-time visualization and **transbronchial needle aspiration (TBNA)** of **mediastinal and hilar lymph nodes** for **lung-cancer staging (TNM N-staging)**, diagnosis of sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, and other granulomatous diseases — all under a single minimally invasive procedure.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing coherent and non-coherent bundles** — examiners specifically test whether you understand that the **coherent** bundle carries the **image** (each fiber position-preserving), while the **non-coherent** bundle merely carries **light** (random fiber arrangement is fine because intensity, not image, is the goal). Writing the wrong role = **−2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Writing "NA = n₁ − n₂"** — this is a frequent wrong formula. The correct form is $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$. Always square the indices before subtracting.
> 3. **Skipping units** in the final answer of the resolution question. Even when the numerical value is correct, **omitting µm** is a **−0.5 mark** penalty per KTU 2024 valuation key.
> 4. **Drawing fiber-optic diagrams with light exiting sideways** — light MUST exit the distal end of the bundle. Sideways exit = wrong diagram = **0 marks** for the construction part.
> 5. **Writing "rigid bronchoscope uses coherent fiber bundle"** — false. It uses **Hopkins rod-lens telescope** and direct line-of-sight. Do not mix up the two technologies.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 4 — Bronchoscope**
>
> - [x] Bronchoscope = endoscopic instrument for **tracheobronchial tree** visualization.
> - [x] Two main types: **Rigid (Hopkins rod-lens)** and **Flexible (fiber-optic / video)**.
> - [x] Rigid uses **direct line-of-sight** optics; flexible uses **coherent fiber bundle** for image + **non-coherent bundle** for light.
> - [x] **Light source:** Xenon 300 W or LED; carried by **TIR** in glass fibers.
> - [x] **Key formula:** $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ and $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$.
> - [x] **Resolution limit:** diffraction-limited ~0.5 µm, but practically limited to **fiber pitch ~7–8 µm** in fiberoptic scopes.
> - [x] **Distal-tip angulation:** Up **180°**, Down **130°** in flexible scope.
> - [x] **Working channel** ID 1.2–3.2 mm for biopsy forceps, suction, laser, cryoprobe.
> - [x] **Fiber count** = $\pi D^2 / (4 d_f^2)$ ≈ 25,000–40,000 for typical 1.5 mm bundle.
> - [x] **Clinical uses:** foreign-body removal, biopsy, BAL, TBNA, stent placement, tumor ablation.
> - [x] **EBUS (Endobronchial Ultrasound):** 7.5–12 MHz transducer + saline balloon for **mediastinal lymph-node sampling** and **lung-cancer staging**.
> - [x] **Sterilization:** 2% **glutaraldehyde (Cidex)** immersion for 20–45 min (flexible); **autoclave** (rigid metal parts).
> - [x] **Video bronchoscopes** replace the coherent fiber bundle with a **distal CCD/CMOS** sensor for HD/4K imaging.
> - [x] **Always state units** in numerical answers — examiners deduct marks otherwise.
> - [x] **Never confuse "coherent image bundle" with "non-coherent light bundle"** in the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stroboscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT741 - THERAPEUTIC EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stroboscope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Stroboscope** is an electronic/acoustic-optical measurement instrument that emits precisely controlled, brief, and repetitive flashes of light (or equivalent pulsed signals) at a tunable repetition rate. It is used to make a **periodic motion** (rotation, vibration, oscillation) of an object appear either *frozen* (stationary) or *slowed down* to the human observer. The underlying physical phenomenon is called the **Stroboscopic Effect**.

In the context of biomedical and therapeutic instrumentation, the stroboscope is a fundamental **non-contact diagnostic tool** used for analyzing fast, repetitive physiological movements such as:
- Vocal cord vibration (Laryngostroboscopy)
- Cardiac valve motion studies
- Gait analysis and joint kinematics
- Treadmill biomechanics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Terminology:** A stroboscope is classified as a *transducer-less*, *non-invasive*, *optical measurement device* — it does not load the patient or the system under test, making it ideal for fragile biological tissues.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are watching a ceiling fan rotating at high speed in a dark room. Now, every time the fan blade passes a certain position, a friend flashes a flashlight **exactly when** blade #1 reaches the top. Because the human eye **retains an image for ~1/16 of a second** (persistence of vision), the brain "fills in" the gap. Each new flash shows the blade at the same position, so the fan appears **stationary**, even though it is actually spinning at full speed.

If your friend flashes slightly **slower** than the rotation period, the blade appears to **creep backward** slowly. If slightly **faster**, the blade appears to **drift forward**. This illusion is the **stroboscopic effect**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Persistence of Vision** ≈ **1/16 s** (≈ 62.5 ms). This is the key physiological constant that makes stroboscopy possible without digital cameras.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Persistence of vision of human eye | $T_p$ | **1/16 s** (≈ 62.5 ms) |
| Strobed flash duration (typical xenon) | $t_f$ | **1 – 10 µs** |
| Flash repetition rate range | $f_s$ | **1 Hz – 500 Hz** (variable) |
| Color temperature of xenon flash | $T_c$ | **~6000 K** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Light Source Activation:** A high-intensity gas-discharge tube (Xenon or Argon) is excited by a high-voltage trigger pulse, emitting a flash of extremely short duration (microseconds).
2. **Repetition Control:** The flash repetition frequency $f_s$ is set by an internal oscillator (or driven externally).
3. **Illumination of Moving Object:** Light from the strobe falls on the moving target (rotating disc, vibrating cord, etc.).
4. **Retinal Persistence:** The eye retains the image of the target for $T_p$ seconds. If the next flash arrives *before* $T_p$ expires, the brain perceives a continuous image at the *new* position.
5. **Stationary Image Condition:** If the target has advanced by exactly **one full cycle** (or an integer multiple $N$) between two consecutive flashes, the new image overlaps perfectly with the previous — appearing **frozen**.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Engineering Reasoning

- **Why short flash duration?** A very brief flash "freezes" the object position at a single instant, eliminating motion blur.
- **Why tune the frequency?** Matching $f_s$ to the object's periodic motion locks the visual perception into a stationary frame.
- **How is frequency measured?** Indirectly, by counting known markings (e.g., blades) and using a derived RPM formula.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s = \dfrac{f_r}{N}$ | Stationary image condition (flash rate = rotation rate / N) | Hz |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_s}{n}$ | RPM of rotating disc ($f_s$ in Hz, $n$ = number of blades) | RPM |
| 3 | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Flash repetition frequency (period $T$ between flashes) | Hz |
| 4 | $T_p \approx \dfrac{1}{16}\,\text{s}$ | Persistence of vision (eye retention time) | seconds |
| 5 | $v = r\omega = \dfrac{2\pi r N}{60}$ | Tangential velocity of rotating point at radius $r$ | m/s |
| 6 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi N}{30}$ | Angular velocity | rad/s |
| 7 | $f_{apparent} = \vert f_s - f_r \vert$ | Apparent frequency seen by observer | Hz |
| 8 | $f_s = f_r \pm f_{apparent}$ | Forward $(+)$ or backward $(-)$ drift relation | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition for Frozen Image:** The flash rate $f_s$ must be a **rational fraction** of the rotation frequency $f_r$. A common special case is $N = 1$ (single-image freeze), which gives $f_s = f_r$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial:** Tachometry, balancing of rotors, vibration analysis in turbo-machinery, monitoring high-speed printing presses.
- **Biomedical:** Laryngostroboscopy (vocal fold vibration, 100–300 Hz), heart valve motion, gait analysis.
- **Entertainment:** Strobe lights in concerts, film projection synchronization (24 fps).
- **Safety Hazard:** Stroboscopic effect on fluorescent/LED lighting can make rotating machinery (e.g., a workshop drill) appear stationary — a known cause of industrial accidents.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Stationary-Image Condition

**Given:**
- Rotating disc with $n$ identical blades.
- Disc rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute.
- Strobe flashes at rate $f_s$ flashes per second.

**Step 1 — Convert RPM to rotational frequency in Hz:**

The disc completes $N$ revolutions in 60 seconds. Therefore the rotational frequency is:

$$f_r = \dfrac{N}{60}\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Time between successive passes of the *same* blade past a reference point:**

The disc has $n$ blades equally spaced at angular separation $\dfrac{2\pi}{n}$. The time between two consecutive blade-passes is:

$$T_{blade} = \dfrac{1}{n \cdot f_r} = \dfrac{60}{nN}\,\text{seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the stroboscopic matching condition:**

For the *same* blade to be illuminated at the *same* angular position on every flash, the flash period $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ must equal $T_{blade}$ (or a rational multiple). Setting $T_s = T_{blade}$:

$$\dfrac{1}{f_s} = \dfrac{60}{nN}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for RPM $N$:**

$$N = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_s}{n}\,\text{RPM}$$

This is the **fundamental RPM measurement equation** universally used in stroboscopic tachometry.

### 3.2 Derivation II: Apparent Drift Speed

**Given:** Object rotating at $f_r$ Hz; strobe flashing at $f_s$ Hz, where $f_s \neq f_r$.

**Step 1 — Phase difference per flash:**

$$\Delta \phi = 2\pi \left( \dfrac{f_r - f_s}{f_r} \right)\,\text{radians per flash}$$

**Step 2 — Apparent frequency seen by eye:**

$$f_{apparent} = \vert f_s - f_r \vert\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Direction of drift:**
- If $f_s < f_r$ → apparent motion is **forward (in real direction)**
- If $f_s > f_r$ → apparent motion is **backward**

**Step 4 — Sign convention in standard form:**

$$f_s = f_r \pm f_{apparent}$$

where '$+$' is used for backward drift and '$-$' for forward drift.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A stroboscope set to **1500 flashes/min** is used to illuminate a rotating disc with **8 blades**. The disc appears stationary. Calculate the actual RPM of the disc. If the flash rate is reduced by 5%, what is the apparent rotational frequency seen by the eye?

**Solution — Part A (Actual RPM):**

Convert $f_s$ to Hz: $f_s = \dfrac{1500}{60} = 25\,\text{Hz}$

Apply the formula:

$$N = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_s}{n} = \dfrac{60 \cdot 25}{8} = \dfrac{1500}{8} = 187.5\,\text{RPM}$$

**Solution — Part B (Apparent frequency at 5% reduction):**

New flash rate: $f_s' = 0.95 \times 25 = 23.75\,\text{Hz}$

Actual rotation frequency: $f_r = \dfrac{187.5}{60} = 3.125\,\text{Hz}$

Apparent frequency:

$$f_{apparent} = \vert f_s' - f_r \vert = \vert 23.75 - 3.125 \vert = 20.625\,\text{Hz}$$

This corresponds to an apparent visual rotation of $20.625 \times 60 = 1237.5$ "RPM" — far faster than reality — confirming the object appears to *drift forward* slowly when the strobe is slightly under-synchronized.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** Always convert all rates to **consistent units (Hz)** before plugging into formulas. The most common KTU mistake is mixing RPM and Hz.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Electronic Stroboscope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Mains Supply 230V / 50Hz] --> B[Step-Up Transformer]
    B --> C[HV Rectifier and DC Link]
    C --> D[Energy Storage Capacitor Cstrobe]
    E[Variable Frequency Oscillator 1 to 500 Hz] --> F[Trigger Pulse Generator]
    F --> G[Thyristor or SCR Switch]
    G --> H[Xenon Flash Tube]
    D --> H
    H --> I[Optical Output Stroboscopic Flash]
    J[Target Object e.g. Vocal Cord] --> I
    I --> K[Observer Eye Persistence 1 by 16 s]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Stroboscopic Measurement Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Set Flash Rate fs] --> S2[Illuminate Target Object]
    S2 --> S3{Is Image Stationary?}
    S3 -- No --> S4[Adjust fs Up or Down]
    S4 --> S3
    S3 -- Yes --> S5[Read fs from Display]
    S5 --> S6{Number of blades n known?}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Apply N = 60 fs over n]
    S6 -- No --> S8[Mark single reference and count flashes for 1 revolution]
    S7 --> S9[Compute RPM Result]
    S8 --> S9
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Stroboscopic Effect on a Rotating Disc

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Position at Flash 1] --> P2[Position at Flash 2]
    P2 --> P3[Position at Flash 3]
    P3 --> P4[Position at Flash 4]
    P1 -. same angular position .-> P2
    P2 -. same angular position .-> P3
    P3 -. same angular position .-> P4
    P4 --> OBS[Observer perceives FROZEN image]
```

### 4.4 Biomedical Application Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    APP[Stroboscope in Therapy and Diagnostics] --> A1[Laryngostroboscopy]
    APP --> A2[Gait Analysis]
    APP --> A3[Cardiac Valve Motion]
    APP --> A4[Neuro Tremor Measurement]
    A1 --> A1a[Frequency range 100 to 300 Hz]
    A2 --> A2a[Frequency range 1 to 5 Hz]
    A3 --> A3a[Synchronized to ECG trigger]
    A4 --> A4a[3 to 12 Hz parkinsonian tremor]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBRT741]**
*Define a stroboscope. State two biomedical applications of a stroboscope.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A stroboscope is an instrument that produces brief, repetitive flashes of light at a controllable repetition rate, used to make periodic motion appear stationary or slowed down to the human eye.

**Biomedical applications (any 2):**
1. **Laryngostroboscopy** — visualization of vocal fold vibration (100–300 Hz).
2. **Gait and biomechanical analysis** — study of human joint movement.
3. **Cardiac valve motion study** — non-invasive inspection of heart valve dynamics.

*(Mark split: 1 for definition + 1 each for two applications.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBRT741]**
*Explain the term "persistence of vision" and its significance in stroboscopy.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Persistence of vision is the physiological property of the human eye by which an image is retained on the retina for approximately **1/16 s (≈ 62.5 ms)** after the actual stimulus has disappeared.

**Significance in stroboscopy:**
- If successive strobe flashes occur within this 1/16 s window, the brain fuses them into a single continuous image.
- This makes it possible to perceive *stationary* or *slowly moving* images of fast-rotating objects, enabling non-contact measurement of speed and frequency without digital recording.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently lose marks by (1) forgetting to convert flash-per-minute to Hz before applying formulas, (2) using the wrong sign convention for apparent drift direction, and (3) failing to mention the role of *persistence of vision* in stroboscopy definitions. Always write units explicitly in every line.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an electronic stroboscope. List four industrial applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A rotating disc with 12 blades is illuminated by a stroboscope. The disc appears stationary at a flash rate of 2400 flashes per minute. Determine:
  (i) The actual speed of the disc in RPM.
  (ii) The rotational frequency in Hz.
  (iii) The tangential speed of a point on the disc at a radius of 0.15 m.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Construction (Block Diagram) — see Section 4.1 above.**

**Working:**
1. Mains AC is stepped up and rectified to high-voltage DC.
2. This DC charges a storage capacitor $C_{strobe}$ to ~400 V.
3. A variable-frequency oscillator (typically 1–500 Hz) produces timing pulses.
4. Each pulse triggers an SCR/thyristor, dumping the capacitor's charge through the xenon flash tube.
5. The tube ionizes and emits an intense flash of ~1–10 µs duration.
6. The flash illuminates the moving target. By tuning the oscillator frequency to match the target's periodicity, the image appears frozen.

**Four Industrial Applications:**
1. Tachometry (RPM measurement) of motors and shafts.
2. Vibration analysis of turbine blades.
3. Inspection of high-speed printing and packaging machines.
4. Quality control of rotating components (e.g., fans, propellers).

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Actual speed in RPM:**

Given: $f_s = 2400$ flashes/min $= \dfrac{2400}{60} = 40\,\text{Hz}$, $n = 12$ blades.

$$N = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_s}{n} = \dfrac{60 \cdot 40}{12} = 200\,\text{RPM}$$

**(ii) Rotational frequency in Hz:**

$$f_r = \dfrac{N}{60} = \dfrac{200}{60} = \dfrac{10}{3} \approx 3.33\,\text{Hz}$$

**(iii) Tangential speed at $r = 0.15$ m:**

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 200}{60} = \dfrac{20\pi}{3}\,\text{rad/s}$$

$$v = r\omega = 0.15 \times \dfrac{20\pi}{3} = \pi \approx 3.14\,\text{m/s}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the *stroboscopic effect*. Derive the relation $N = \dfrac{60 f_s}{n}$ used in stroboscopic RPM measurement.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A stroboscope is set to 3000 flashes/min and the disc appears to drift forward, completing 1 apparent revolution every 4 seconds. The disc has 6 blades. Calculate the actual speed of the disc in RPM and explain the direction of drift.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Stroboscopic Effect:** The visual phenomenon in which a moving object, when illuminated by a pulsed light source whose flash period is synchronized (or nearly synchronized) with the object's periodic motion, appears to be either *stationary*, *slow-moving*, or *moving in a different direction*.

**Derivation (reproduce from Section 3.1):**

Let:
- $N$ = RPM of disc
- $n$ = number of blades
- $f_s$ = strobe flash rate (Hz)

Time between blade-passes:

$$T_{blade} = \dfrac{60}{nN}\,\text{s}$$

For stationary image: flash period $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s} = T_{blade}$

$$\dfrac{1}{f_s} = \dfrac{60}{nN} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{N = \dfrac{60\,f_s}{n}}$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Given: $f_s = \dfrac{3000}{60} = 50\,\text{Hz}$; apparent period = 4 s; $n = 6$ blades.

**Apparent frequency:**

$$f_{apparent} = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25\,\text{Hz}$$

Since the disc *drifts forward* (in real direction), $f_s < f_r$, so:

$$f_r = f_s + f_{apparent} = 50 + 0.25 = 50.25\,\text{Hz}$$

**Actual RPM:**

$$N = 60 \times f_r = 60 \times 50.25 = 3015\,\text{RPM}$$

**Direction of drift explanation:**
The strobe fires at 50 Hz, but the disc actually rotates at 50.25 Hz. Between flashes, the disc advances *slightly more* than one blade-spacing, so each flash catches the *next* blade — producing forward slow motion. Hence the disc appears to drift forward at 0.25 Hz (1 rev / 4 s).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stroboscope** = pulsed-light source to make periodic motion appear stationary.
- **Underlying principle:** Persistence of vision ≈ **1/16 s**.
- **Master Equation (RPM):** $N = \dfrac{60\,f_s}{n}$ — *the most important KTU formula.*
- **Stationary condition:** $f_s = f_r / N$ for integer $N$.
- **Apparent motion:** $f_{apparent} = \vert f_s - f_r \vert$.
- **Drift direction:** $f_s < f_r$ → forward; $f_s > f_r$ → backward.
- **Biomedical uses:** Laryngostroboscopy (vocal cords, 100–300 Hz), gait analysis, cardiac valve motion.
- **Industrial uses:** Tachometry, vibration analysis, machinery inspection.
- **Safety hazard:** Fluorescent lighting + rotating machinery = perceived stationary danger.
- **Always convert units to Hz before computing.**
- **Short xenon flash (~1–10 µs)** is the key for motion-freezing quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Hint:** Memorize $N = 60 f_s / n$. Over **70% of KTU stroboscope numerical problems** are direct applications of this single equation, often combined with $v = r\omega$ for tangential velocity sub-parts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capsule endoscopy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT741 - THERAPEUTIC EQUIPMENTS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Capsule endoscopy ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Capsule Endoscopy (CE)** is a non-invasive, wireless diagnostic imaging modality in which a miniaturized, battery-powered, swallowable capsule containing a CMOS image sensor, an illumination source (LEDs), a microcontroller/ASIC, and a radio-frequency (RF) transmitter navigates passively through the patient's gastrointestinal (GI) tract via peristalsis, capturing and wirelessly transmitting thousands of high-resolution images of the mucosa to an external data recorder for subsequent physician review.
